Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

title: Defining Novel Roles of Ubiquitin Accumulation During the Mammalian Oxidative Stress Response
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Environmental exposures can cause the accumulation of reactive oxygen species, which will lead to many diseases such as cancer and neurodegeneration. However, fundamental knowledge on how cells respond to this environmental stress is not fully understood. This proposal aims to carefully study how ubiquitin signaling participates in this oxidative stress response in humans, to promote new methods of mitigating disease progression.

application_id: 11049329
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the mechanism of herpesvirus genome packaging
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Herpesviruses are important human pathogens that can have devastating effects in neonates and immunocompromised individuals, yet there is no cure for any herpesvirus and treatment options are lacking. Here, I dissect the mechanism of an essential feature of the herpesviral lifecycle - viral genome packaging - by exploring the role of a unique accessory factor required for packaging and by developing an assay to monitor packaging in vitro. Detailed mechanistic insight is essential to understand the mechanism of current antiherpesviral drugs and to facilitate the identification of new targets for antivirals.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Antibiotic resistant bacteria are a growing global threat to human health and wellbeing. I propose to
investigate how the spatiotemporal organization and physiology of gut bacteria could be targeted to stop the
emergence and spread of antibiotic resistance. This work will inform the design of new strategies for
combating antibiotic resistance through the therapeutic manipulation of the intestinal microbiome’s physical
architecture.

activity_code: F30
title: Establishing sex-specific mechanisms for estrogen receptor beta in heroin extinction memory recall
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In recent years, rapid increases in the prevalence of opioid use disorder have uncovered sex differences in opioid use, such as a predisposition to opioid use in females that can negatively impact treatment outcomes. This is particularly concerning, as females have often been excluded from research studies on reward processing and addiction. This proposal aims to address this research gap by describing the mechanisms by which estrogen impacts heroin seeking, knowledge that could help tailor sex-specific interventions for opioid use disorder.

application_id: 10923485
activity_code: DP2
title: Discovery of bacterial defense and phage counter-defense strategies
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The interplay between anti-phage defense systems in bacteria and counter-defense strategies in phages can determine phage infection outcomes, which in turn have widespread impacts on the evolution and treatment of infectious disease. Most defense and counter-defense systems in nature have not been identified, so we will apply a novel functional selection scheme to identify these systems from various metagenomes and libraries of synthesized phage genes. We will then use genetic and functional techniques to characterize these systems mechanistically and reveal fundamental insights into the molecular arms race between bacteria and phage.

application_id: 10849231
activity_code: T32
title: NRSA Training Grant in Quantitative Neuroscience
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: This is an application for support for advanced predoctoral and postdoctoral training in quantitative neuroscience. By instructing our trainees in computational modeling and advanced data analysis techniques, we aim to train scientists that will be able to bridge the key gap in our understanding of how the brain works: how do myriad single molecules and neurons collectively give rise to the mind? This is important for both basic science and clinical science - these modeling and data analysis techniques will give trainees a rich vocabulary and a powerful toolkit for exploring how pathologies of small-scale elements (e.g., deficits in neurotransmission at dopaminergic terminals) can lead to mental disorders (e.g., schizophrenia).

application_id: 11195541
activity_code: DP5
title: Spatial Regulation of Epigenetic Memory
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: The mechanistic relationship between 3D genome folding, chromatin dynamics, and stability of epigenetic
memory is unclear due to challenges of simultaneously measuring these parameters in single cells. As such,
we have a limited understanding of how chromatin folding impacts epigenetic stability and how this effects
development processes and disease. Drawing on recent advances in multiplexed microscopy and super
resolution imaging, I aim to develop new imaging technologies to study the dynamic feedback between

application_id: 10897335
activity_code: F30
title: Minor Histocompatibility Antigen T Cell Targeting in Acute Myeloid Leukemia
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is the deadliest leukemia, and though treatment with allogeneic stem cell transplantation (alloSCT) and other immunotherapies is often beneficial, over 50% of AML patients eventually relapse. Little is known about what the drivers of the graft versus host and graft versus leukemia effects of alloSCT in this context. We hypothesize that minor histocompatibility antigens (mHAs) are a key driver of the effects of alloSCT for AML, and seek to identify novel mHAs and assess mHA-specific T cells in post-alloSCT relapse.

activity_code: T32
title: Genetics and Genomics of Human Disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Rapid and inexpensive DNA sequencing technologies have revolutionized medicine by revealing the landscape of mutations that contribute to human disease. Over the next decade, whole genome sequencing of many thousands of individuals, coupled with novel genome editing approaches to determine the impact of specific human mutations in experimental model systems, will provide unprecedented insight into disease mechanisms and empower new strategies for prevention, diagnosis and treatment. This training program will train the next generation of scientists to be able to take full advantage of this sea change in human genetics and prepare them for productive careers in science that will lead to improved human health.

title: Study of the Development and Function of the Uterine Lymphatics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative The proposed research project aims to characterize the development and functions of the murine uterine lymphatics: we hypothesize that the uterine lymphatics develop postnatally under molecular patterning guidance from uterine blood vessels and functionally, they maintain fluid homeostasis within the uterine environment throughout estrous phases and pregnancy and are essential for successful embryonic implantation and gestation. This study has the potential to not only address the dearth of academic knowledge on uterine lymphatics but also provide novel understanding of the basis of uterine fertility and disease from the lymphatic perspective.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There is a critical need for more physician scientists engaged in ophthalmology and vision research. This research training program is intended to increase the number of medical students who have interest and experience in research relevant to ophthalmology and vision science. Through a combination of direct firsthand mentored research training, didactic activities, career development, and networking activities, this program will expand the ophthalmology research workforce to better meet the needs of an increasing population of patients suffering from ophthalmic conditions.

application_id: 11160702
activity_code: K23
title: Improving Community-level Bystander Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation in Underserved Populations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE—RELEVANCE TO HEALTH
 Bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation (B-CPR) is the most effective treatment for out-of-hospital cardiac
arrest (OHCA); however, the likelihood of receiving B-CPR and surviving OHCA varies significantly based on
neighborhood demographics. Our research is designed to identify modifiable barriers to B-CPR participation in
at-risk neighborhoods, and adapt a targeted, culturally sensitive, community-level intervention designed to
improve B-CPR participation and OHCA survival in underserved populations.

title: ALIVE: Development and feasibility of a psychosocial intervention for sexual and gender minority autistic adults
org_name: HUNTER COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Autistic sexual and gender minority adults are at high risk for co-occurring mental health conditions including depression, anxiety, and suicide attempts and require a psychosocial intervention tailored for their unique needs. application_id: 10897940
activity_code: F32
title: How does the ubiquitously expressed ZFX mediate cell type-specific transcriptional regulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: ZFX is a transcriptional activator with large effects on the human transcriptome in multiple cell lines. Despite preliminary data from the Farnham Lab that demonstrates almost complete overlap of ZFX binding to thousands of promoter regions in several cell lines, the sets of genes activated by ZFX are surprisingly unique to each cell line. To determine how ZFX regulates cell type-specific gene expression while binding to the same set of promoters, this proposal will identify ZFX protein binding partners and other transcription factors bound to promoters of ZFX regulated genes in multiple cell lines.

title: Nutrition, Obesity and Atherosclerosis Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Over-nutrition and obesity are important contributors to the cardiovascular disease epidemic in the United States and worldwide. A major reason for the increase in cardiovascular disease is the rapidly increasing prevalence of obesity in both adults and children, many of whom will develop cardiovascular disease, and so the burden on our society is tremendous. The Nutrition, Obesity and Atherosclerosis Training Program trains new generations of postdoctoral MD clinician-scientists and PhD scientists to tackle these problems, taking advantage of the multidisciplinary and broad training environment at the University of Washington.

application_id: 10949705
activity_code: K99
title: Non-invasive brain stimulation and neurovascular mechanisms of post-stroke cognitive impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA HLTH SCIENCES CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:  One of the devastating, yet understudied outcomes of stroke is a persistent deterioration of cognitive abilities in stroke survivors. Previous studies and my preliminary data suggest that transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) indicate that this non-invasive brain stimulation methodology may provide an important therapeutic approach to combat post-stroke cognitive impairment. Further, my research proposal will explore understudied mechanisms by which tDCS exerts its effect on cognitive function in stroke survivors.

application_id: 10950224
activity_code: K01
title: Evaluation of clinical and social factors associated with deficit accumulation trajectories in older survivors with early-stage non-small cell lung cancer
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project narrative Aging involves a gradual clinical decline in cognitive and physical function and often the development of various comorbidities. However, significant life events, such as a cancer diagnosis, cancer treatment, or other individual-level and societal-level factors can accelerate clinical decline and aging in cancer survivors compared to other older people without a cancer diagnosis. The completion of this study will identify targetable multilevel aging risk factors in the older population with lung cancer, as well as provide a valuable monitoring tool to predict dynamic risk of aging-related clinical decline, allowing for a deeper understanding of drivers of aging acceleration and timely intervention before clinical decline occurs.

application_id: 10849662
activity_code: F30
title: Nanobodies for Dissecting the Structure and Function of Oligomeric BAX
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE BCL-2 family proteins are essential arbiters of programmed cell death during development and homeostasis and when deregulated contribute to the development, maintenance, and chemoresistance of human cancers. A critical missing link in our understanding of the apoptotic pathway is how BAX, an essential executioner protein, self-assembles into higher-order oligomers to permeabilize the mitochondrial outer membrane. The goal of my research is to harness cutting-edge nanobody technology and the first homogeneous species of oligomeric BAX to provide new structural insight into the execution phase of BAX-mediated apoptosis and thereby inform new opportunities to modulate BAX for therapeutic benefit in cancer.

Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a disorder affecting 30 million Americans and is thought to arise in part from
problems in the way the brain and gut interact. This Core will provide innovative programs to enhance
collaboration, training, education and recruitment of new investigators, and community outreach. It is therefore

activity_code: F31
title: Genetic and molecular mechanisms of Xbp-1 mediated salivary gland development and differentiation
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative  The development, differentiation and functional maturation of the salivary gland is a tightly regulated process that requires the coordinated activity of important transcriptional networks and signaling pathways. This application focuses on examining the gene-regulatory mechanisms, and in particular the role of the transcription factor Xbp-1, in directing morphogenesis, cellular identity and fate. Such studies will lead to an improved understanding of the inner workings of the salivary gland and can provide molecular insights into regeneration processes that can help patients with salivary gland dysfunction.

title: Neoantigen-specific T cells in a novel cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma model
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Non-melanoma skin cancer, including cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), is the most common cancer. This proposal uses a novel physiologic cSCC murine model to address the critical need to understand the role of CD8 and CD4 T cells, especially the role of neoantigen-specific T cells, in controlling cSCC growth. Completion of this proposal will determine the features of neoantigen-specific T cell responses that constrain cSCC growth and identify features of cancer vaccination strategies that improve outcomes in cSCC and enhance response to anti-PD-1.

application_id: 10951512
activity_code: F32
title: Identifying genetic vulnerabilities in KIAA1549-BRAF mutant pediatric low-grade gliomas
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: Project Narrative The most frequent genomic alteration in pediatric low-grade gliomas is a rearrangement that creates a fusion between the oncogene BRAF and the uncharacterized protein KIAA1549. Inhibitors which block the function of this fusion are effective but cause significant toxicity and require continuous dosing throughout childhood. The goal of this project is to define new therapeutic targets in KIAA1549-BRAF mutant pLGGs, with a particular focus on characterizing how the POMT complex regulates KIAA1549-BRAF activity.

title: Identifying human-specific neural progenitors and their role in neurodevelopment
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The human central nervous system (CNS) is characterized by an increase in cell diversity as well as size, features which underlie our amazing cognitive and behavioral capacity. Neural progenitor cells directly dictate both the size and cell diversity of the CNS; understanding how the human neural progenitor cell population differs from that of mice or monkeys is therefore fundamental to understanding what makes our nervous systems unique. title: The Respiratory Microbiome in COVID-19: Associations with Severity, Risk Factors, and Host Pathways
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: COVID-19 is characterized by heterogenous symptoms and outcomes, and identifying what factors determine disease severity is a top priority. The airway microbiome has emerged as a differentiating factor, with a less diverse, dysbiotic microbiome found early in more severe disease. This work aims to carry these associations to the species level, understand how comorbidities influence the microbiome, and leverage advanced machine learning to start identifying potential microbe-host interaction mechanisms.

application_id: 10849790
activity_code: K08
title: Molecular mechanisms underlying isoflurane conditioning-induced neurovascular protection in subarachnoid hemorrhage
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) carries very significant mortality and morbidity, with nearly half of SAH survivors having major cognitive dysfunction that impairs their functional status, emotional health, and quality of life. Much of this is due to Delayed Cerebral Ischemia (DCI) and the present study aims to understand the underlying mechanisms of anesthetic conditioning-induced protection against DCI. Through the proposed experiments, it is likely that new pathophysiologic mechanisms will be determined and new therapeutic targets identified that may ultimately lead to improved patient outcome after SAH.

application_id: 10899598
activity_code: DP2
title: A modular cell therapy platform for controlling immunological tolerance
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In the United States, more than 24 million people suffer from autoimmune disease and over 600,000 people die of cancer each year. These maladies are predominantly a consequence of inappropriate orchestration of immunological tolerance, yet few therapies exist to precisely control the associated processes. In this project, we develop an entirely new class of living cell therapies that home to sites of tolerance induction and reprogram immune responses to fight cancer and quell autoimmunity.

org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY

Depression and anxiety are marked by differences in emotion processing that may originate in preschool
emotional neurodevelopment. This project aims to characterize how early caregiving and negative affect

well positioned to start their independent research program and have strong support from their department and
broader university.

activity_code: T32
title: Diabetes Mellitus and Disorders of Metabolism
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Obesity, diabetes and osteoporosis are increaingly prevalent metabolic diseases that are reponsible for a great
burden of suffering and death as well as large healthcare expenditures in the US and throughout the world.
Academic endocrine researchers are needed to better understand these diseases and to translate research
findings into new and improved therapies. The Yale T32 program in Diabetes and Disorders of Metabolism is
dedicated to providing young graduate students, physicians and scientists with the research training necessary
to tackle these important public health problems.

application_id: 10899683
activity_code: K08
title: Antiretroviral Therapy Impacts Autophagy in Astrocytes, and May Contribute to HIV Associated Neurocognitive Disorders
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative HIV associated neurocognitive disorders (HAND), a highly burdensome comorbid condition of HIV infection, continues to plague people with HIV (PWH) even in the current antiretroviral therapy (ART) era. Efforts to treat HAND are hindered by an incomplete understanding of the factors underlying its development. This proposal will examine the impact of a common antiretroviral regimen on autophagy, an essential process for homeostasis in astrocytes, the major support cells of the brain, as a pathogenic contribution to HAND to identify potential therapeutic targets for HAND.

application_id: 11064033
activity_code: K08
title: Neuronal Seizure Burden versus Cell Death after Neonatal Brain Injury
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Neonatal hypoxic-ischemic brain injury is the leading cause of neonatal seizures, for which treatment is both
ineffective and potentially harmful. The immediate goals of this proposal are to develop two-photon imaging
methods for in vivo real-time seizure and cell death monitoring and to define the relationship between neuronal
seizure burden and the probability of cell death in a neonatal murine model of hypoxic-ischemic brain injury.
The experiments proposed will generate rigorous correlational data between seizure burden and the probability
of neuronal death with the necessary temporal and spatial resolution to make possible future studies of
causation, which are essential to determine how aggressively to treat patients and how to prioritize future
research efforts to ...

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DELAWARE
phr: Project Narrative 
While Delaware has high quality healthcare compared to other states, it still lags behind other states in a number of areas, including drug-related deaths, obesity, high preventable hospitalization rates, multiple chronic conditions and cancer. As such, the overarching goal for the continuation of the ACCEL CTR Program is to create a unified Delaware Clinical and Translational Research Community (inclusive of investigators and community members) that breaks down existing barriers, capitalizes on the unique strengths of its partners to establish infrastructure and accelerates the creation of solutions that improve the health of all Delawareans and all Americans

application_id: 11057692
activity_code: K23
title: Epidemiology and Biomarkers in Transplant Associated Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TA-TMA): A Prospective Validation Cohort Study
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy (TA-TMA) is an often-fatal hematologic
disorder without an effective treatment currently. This project aims to provide a better
understanding of the epidemiology and risk factors for this rare disease and to design and test a
dynamic risk prediction tool through a prospective cohort study. Results of this proposal will be
helpful to the understanding of the disease and the design of future interventional trials.

application_id: 10899947
activity_code: F30
title: Impact of Species-Specific Synaptic Maturation Timing on Cortical Circuit and Behavioral Development
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Synaptic development in the human brain is strikingly prolonged compared to other mammals, and in humans, this may contribute both to extended periods of learning and increased susceptibility to neurodevelopmental disease. However, the true biological significance and impact of this extended timing on circuit and behavioral maturation is largely unknown. This project will investigate the impact of synaptic developmental timing on the formation of cortical circuits and adult behavior, and this will present a potential novel approach to prevent disease through therapeutic modulation of neurodevelopmental timing.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This study will determine whether the California responsible beverage service (RBS) training law has an impact on alcohol-related motor vehicle crashes, and whether “usual and customary” training will match the efficacy of an online WaytoServe RBS training program in reducing over-service of alcohol to apparently-intoxicated pseudopatrons at licensed on-premises establishments such as bars and restaurants. title: Deciphering the role of cytoskeletal-nuclear interactions in peripheral chromatin organization
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Organization of the mammalian genome is one mechanism that regulates gene expression, cellular identity, and disease. Mutations in the LMNA gene result in altered chromatin organization, nuclear morphology defects, and dilated cardiomyopathy, which is abrogated upon co-deletion of SUN1 in a mouse model. The proposed studies will begin to elucidate the mechanisms underlying how nuclear-cytoskeleton interactions via the LINC complex are critical to the maintenance of genome organization and cellular function in cardiomyocytes.

application_id: 11093595
activity_code: F32
title: Phenotypic sorting of cancer cells to study the role and control of cell stiffness in the in vivo metastatic cascade
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer cells differ in their mechanical properties depending on their metastatic potential when measured in vitro. Understanding the role that a cell’s mechanotype plays in vivo at each step of the metastatic cascade will be critical for developing new diagnostics and therapeutic targets for metastatic disease, the cause of a majority of cancer-related deaths. This proposal seeks to understand the heritability of cell stiffness and the advantage or disadvantage conferred by cell stiffness to metastasis through the combination of high-throughput microfluidic mechanical screening, in vivo mouse metastasis models, and multi-omic analyses.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

cervical lesions; however, few studies have investigated the utility of methylation markers as predictors of
progression from low-grade to high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN-1 to CIN-2/3). This study will
utilize a unique combination of longitudinal outcomes and methylation array data in a prospective clinical cohort
of women to assess relationships between early-stage DNA methylation, high-risk HPV infection, and the
progression or persistence of CIN-1. Study findings will help identify novel methylation markers of cervical
disease progression, improve the clinical management of low-grade cervical precancer, and contribute to our

activity_code: F31
title: Monosynaptic tracing defines circuit connectivity of human high-grade glioma in the adult mouse brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative As the most common primary brain malignancy, glioblastoma (GBM) carries poor prognosis and insights into tumor biology in the context of the normal brain environment are desperately needed to define new therapeutic strategies. Using a patient-derived GBM organoid model, this proposal seeks to repurpose monosynaptic rabies viral tracing in the adult mouse brain for understanding the circuit connectivity of GBM, aided by a combination of calcium imaging, optogenetics, electrophysiology, and RNA sequencing. Our findings will define the diverse brain circuitry that interacts with GBM and harness this knowledge to propose novel therapeutic targets for this disease, such as the metabotropic cholinergic receptors.

application_id: 10903928
activity_code: F31
title: Probing silent cerebral infarct pathogenesis in sickle cell disease with cerebrovascular MRI
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE One of the most prevalent problems in children with sickle cell disease are silent cerebral infarcts, small regions of the brain where cells are injured or dead. Silent cerebral infarcts lead to serious problems such as future strokes and learning disabilities, but we do not currently understand why they happen and therefore do not have good ways of preventing them. This proposal will use MRI to measure blood flow throughout the brain in children with sickle cell disease to understand how changes in blood supply may be related to the development of silent cerebral infarcts.

application_id: 10901275
activity_code: F30
title: The role of CCR6 in Pancreatic Cancer and Tregs
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Avoiding immune destruction is a hallmark of cancer that is especially prominent within pancreatic cancer tumors (10,11). The proposed studies will investigate of a population of highly functional regulatory T cells to determine their mechanisms of immune suppression and their impact in pancreatic cancer. The data generated from these studies will have implications in the study of autoimmune disease and other types of cancer, providing critical information for not only the development of these diseases but also in the design of new therapeutics.

application_id: 10901665
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidation of the Mechanism of Microhomology-Mediated End Joining by DNA Polymerase Theta
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Harmful DNA breaks caused by exposure to radiation, chemicals, or as a result of mistakes that occur when DNA is being duplicated are usually repaired accurately through a process called homologous recombination (HR). When a DNA break occurs in an HR-deficient cell, it triggers a highly error-prone, cancer- inducing backup DNA repair pathway called microhomology-mediated end joining (MMEJ), and cells produce large amounts of the protein that performs MMEJ: DNA polymerase theta (PolQ). My project will uncover the step-by-step mechanism of how PolQ performs MMEJ and inform new strategies for discovering molecules that inhibit PolQ function to be investigated as potential HR-deficient cancer drugs.

application_id: 11063290
activity_code: R61
title: Development of an online mindfulness training program for adults with subjective cognitive decline
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There is a dire need to systematically develop, refine, and examine for efficacy promising behavioral
interventions that can be easily disseminated to aging adults at-risk for AD and related dementias.
The research proposed in the current application will systematically develop and test the feasibility of an
internet-based, asynchronous, mindfulness program (iMBSR) with a matched control group for adults with
subjective cognitive decline. This Stage I study, yielding estimates on successful recruitment, adherence,
adverse events, overall acceptability, and effect sizes for mind-wandering, and plasma-based AD biomarkers
of Aβ and tau will provide important foundational data for future efficacy trials of the iMBSR program.

application_id: 10901424
activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing adherence and estimating per-protocol effects for pediatric dolutegravir use
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adherence to antiretroviral therapy is vital for prevention of AIDS-related deaths and comorbidities in children living with HIV, but pediatric populations face greater challenges to adherence compared to adults including difficulties with medication tolerability. Pediatric trials and evidence generated from these trials have lagged behind adult trials, especially in regard to use of modern causal inference methods to address analytic challenges. This proposal will use data from a pediatric randomized trial of dolutegravir-based ART compared to standard of care and novel epidemiologic methods to demonstrate how to improve evaluation of evidence generated from pediatric trials.

activity_code: F31
title: Role of hypoxia in fibroblast reprogramming in pancreatic cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pancreatic cancer is characterized by a hypoxic microenvironment and a dense, desmoplastic stroma which is
mainly composed of fibroblasts and extracellular matrix. The goal of this proposal is to elucidate the mechanisms
by which hypoxia regulates bidirectional interactions between cancer cells and fibroblasts in pancreatic cancer.
My studies will provide new insights into the impact of hypoxia on tumor-stroma crosstalk in pancreatic cancer,
which can be exploited to devise new stroma-targeting approaches for this disease.

title: Engineering Biomaterials to Modulate the Bone Marrow Microenvironment in Multiple Myeloma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multiple myeloma is a cancer of bone marrow plasma cells that remains virtually incurable, partly to interactions with the bone marrow microenvironment, which promote colonization, progression, and survival of myeloma cells. During the F99 phase, I will develop biomaterials-based strategies to deliver nucleic acids to the bone marrow microenvironment, as a novel form of multiple myeloma therapy, to disrupt interactions between myeloma cells and the bone marrow. Animal studies suggest the effects of cannabis among heavy drinkers may be partially due to the impact of THC on the microbiota-gut-brain axis (MGBA; which refers to the bidirectional communication between the digestive, immune, and central nervous systems), however no human studies have explicitly explored this question. title: Coordinated growth in microbial communities and implications for antibiotic sensitivities
org_name: CARNEGIE INSTITUTION OF WASHINGTON, D.C.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Antibiotic treatment decreases the burden of bacterial diseases, but efficacy is often diminished when bacteria are in mixed microbial communities such as the microbiome. Lactic acid bacteria are a component of the microbiome; however, little is known about how these bacteria impact community physiology and how their growth is affected by small molecules from the community. The proposed study will determine the molecular mechanisms of how the growth of the lactic acid bacteria Lactiplantbacillus plantarum is coordinated and how this influences antibiotic sensitivities and community stability.

application_id: 10902593
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanistic examination and inhibitor targeting of nutrient scavenging for the treatment of pancreatic cancer
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by RAS and autophagy-dependent growth, and dual inhibition of the RAS-ERK MAPK and autophagy pathways is a potential treatment strategy for PDAC. One aim of this study will assess whether prolonged inhibition of the RAS-ERK MAPK pathway results in compensatory upregulation of another nutrient scavenging pathway, macropinocytosis, and whether this process mediates resistance to dual RAS-ERK MAPK and autophagy pathway inhibition. Additionally, I demonstrate that ERK inhibition results in increased macropinocytosis and hypothesize that this could be exploited to increase delivery of albumin-bound therapeutics such as nab-paclitaxel, which is currently second-line treatment for PDAC.

application_id: 10903429
activity_code: F31
title: Lrfn2 as a Novel Resilience Factor to Protect Against Alzheimer's Disease-Related Cortical Neurodegeneration
org_name: JACKSON LABORATORY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Currently, over six million Americans are living with Alzheimer's disease (AD), and by 2050 this number is projected to rise to nearly 13 million if no disease-modifying treatments are discovered. The lack of successful therapeutic options can be attributed to the complexity of AD and the limited understanding of the genetic and neuronal mechanisms that underly AD. I performed genetic mapping of brain-composition traits in a panel of genetically diverse mice that model human AD to identify Lrfn2 as a gene that modifies AD-related cortical neurodegeneration, and in the proposed project I will determine if Lrfn2 is a genuine mediator of resilience to AD and is a valid target for development of effective treatments for AD.

application_id: 10903542
activity_code: F31
title: Integrated metabolomic and microbial community analysis of estuarine sediments and their potential as sources of bioactive natural products
org_name: OREGON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Estuaries are dynamic interfaces between the marine and terrestrial environments and estuarine sediments support a tremendous diversity of microorganisms which interact by expression of chemical signals or natural products. Microbial natural products have been valuable sources of therapeutic agents, and there remains a need for new biologically active compounds to study and treat drug-resistant cancers and infectious diseases. The use of in situ sampling methods paired with advanced mass spectrometric analysis will facilitate an unprecedented metabolomic study of estuarine sediment communities and identification of biologically active natural products with potential for human disease treatment and management.

org_name: MIRIAM HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative Lifestyle factors, including obesity, physical inactivity and smoking, account for more than 50% of the burden of chronic disease morbidity and mortality. application_id: 10902537
activity_code: F31
title: The Impact of Early and Later-life Stress on Later-life Cognition and Plasma Biomarkers of Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias
org_name: WAKE FOREST UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  By 2050, Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) are estimated to cost the US 1.6 trillion dollars, emphasizing the need to understand their contributing factors. Life course stress has been linked to an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, anxiety, and elevated levels of inflammation which have long-lasting negative effects on an individual’s health, but there is limited research on associations between lifespan stress and ADRD. The overarching goal of this proposal is to examine the associations of early and later-life stress on later-life cognition and plasma biomarkers of ADRD pathology and whether associations differ by sex, in an effort to provide insights for targeted interventions to mitigate ADRD risk.

application_id: 10902538
activity_code: F32
title: Designing addressable proteoliposomes by using viral transmembrane proteins and biomimetic cell-free synthesis techniques.
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This research fellowship aims to design a useful tool for both gaining fundamental knowledge of viral protein-cell membrane interactions and addressing current challenges in targeted drug- delivery, especially those associated with carrier (delivery vehicle) -cell receptor specificity. By developing adapted cell-free protein synthesis techniques, we intend to construct user-defined proteoliposomes whose composition can mirror an exterior viral surface, used towards either furthering our understanding of virus ligand-host cell interactions or repurposed for targeted therapies exploiting the native fidelity of virus membrane proteins and for their respective cellular receptors.

application_id: 10902995
activity_code: F31
title: Biomechanics and mechanobiology of human uterine fibroids
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Uterine fibroids are noncancerous tumors that form within the uterus, affecting nearly 70 - 80% of women in their lifetimes, and are a major cause of severe pain, bleeding, and infertility. Yet, despite its high prevalence and significant disease burden, current treatment options are limited, relying primarily on surgical means of removal, with innovation hampered by a poor understanding of the underlying disease pathophysiology. Therefore, this research proposal seeks to establish fundamental knowledge regarding the structure, function, and mechanics of uterine fibroids through a multi-scale, multi-faceted engineering-based approach to directly improve the diagnosis and treatment of uterine fibroids.

title: Emotion Regulation in Children with Developmental Language Disorder
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA AT COLUMBIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Increased mental health problems are present in developmental language disorder (OLD) and emotion regulation difficulties are one potential contributor to their development. This project will investigate the emotion regulation of children with OLD in comparison to children with typical development during both linguistically demanding and domain-general tasks using self-report, behavioral, and physiological indicators. The project will contribute to fundamental knowledge about the ER functioning of children with OLD, inform the multimethod assessment of ER in populations with language deficits, support early identification of emotional problems in children with OLD, and possibly allow for earlier targeted interventions to prevent and mitigate future mental health problems ...

application_id: 10903288
activity_code: F30
title: Genetic determinants of endoplasmic reticulum stress in OI and its consequences on cellular receptors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE OI patients experience brittle bone and recurrent fractures due to mutations impacting type I collagen production, modification, or trafficking. Recent work has identified that misfolded type I collagen accumulates in the ER and causes ER stress in certain individuals with OI -- it is not known if this phenomenon is common to all forms of OI or if the process interferes with bone active therapeutics used to treat OI. This proposal will establish that ER stress as a clinically important, genotype dependent feature of OI and establish preclinical data for a new precision strategy to treat OI.

activity_code: F31
title: Advancing drug discovery for protein domains containing intrinsically disordered regions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Disorder-containing protein domains inherently have a dynamic nature and play important roles in numerous physiological processes as well as pathological states. In this work, I propose an innovative workflow for the high-throughput identification and optimization of modulators for two distinct, dynamic transcriptional coactivator domains, KIX and HEAT, the results of which will directly contribute the cancer therapeutics. This workflow will be pivotal for the successful targeting of conformationally dynamic proteins, a class of proteins that is known to contribute to numerous diseases but is largely considered to be ‘undruggable’.

org_name: UPSTATE MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  The assembly and disassembly of CTD-mediated transcription factory and the molecular interactions that regulate this process are poorly understood. application_id: 10973550
activity_code: F31
title: Rho GTPase regulation in trophoblasts by c-Jun N-terminalkinase signaling
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PRIOJECT NARRATIVE A key step in placental development is the invasion of trophoblast cells into the endometrium of the uterus. Dysregulation of this process can lead to severe pregnancy complications including preeclampsia and miscarriage, but the cell signaling mechanisms controlling the process of trophoblast migration are unclear. This proposal will establish a novel signaling axis governing trophoblast migration, and will shed light on how signaling becomes dysregulated during this process, potentially highlighting targets to be explored in therapies for common pregnancy complications related to aberrant placental development.

title: Coordinated Actions of SNAI1 and AR in Prostate Cancer Dissemination and Metastatic Outgrowth
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In 2019, virtually all 31,620 prostate cancer (PCa) deaths in the United States were attributed to metastasis. application_id: 10903823
activity_code: K23
title: Impact of Health Deductible Health Plans on the Evaluation, Treatment, and Outcomes for Acute Coronary Syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Most US employees have health insurance with greater than $1,000 deductibles, yet little research has studied how high out-of-pocket costs affect patients experiencing acute cardiovascular emergencies such as heart attacks. The proposed research will advance our understanding of high-deductible health plans’ impact on outcomes from heart attacks, unnecessary hospitalizations for chest pain, and patients’ perspectives on how high out-of-pocket costs influence their care decisions and perceived care quality. Results from these studies will provide important implications for future insurance regulations to minimize unintended harm, reduce the financial burden of care, and align clinical decisions with patients’ value perspectives.

application_id: 10903854
activity_code: K99
title: Left-right asymmetry and PITX2 in atrial fibrillation
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Atrial fibrillation is the most common heart rhythm disorder in the United States with 1 in 4 adults at risk of disease development and is linked to serious debilitating complications like stroke and heart failure. For reasons we don’t yet understand, the developmental left/right patterning gene, PITX2, is linked to disease risk and recurrence following intervention late in life. In support of the NIH mission to promote better treatment and prevention strategies, my research study will use recent technological advances to study how PITX2-dependent patterning on the left side of the heart during development is required for a lifetime of normal heart rhythm.

application_id: 10997705
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of TBC1D19 in microtubule dynamics, ciliation and organelle morphology
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Primary cilia are signal transducers important for development, cell polarization, and cell cycle control, and aberrant ciliation has been linked to developmental disorders and cancer. Cilia microtubules are highly glutamylated by the Tubulin Poly-glutamylation Complex, which interacts with TBC1D19, a protein with unknown function. Ablation of TBC1D19 in mammalian cells abrogates ciliation and alters Golgi morphology, prompting us to investigate whether these phenotypes are due to a role for TBC1D19 in promoting glutamylation or a potential function as a GTPase activating protein.

activity_code: F31
title: Microglia-neuron dopamine signaling - a novel mechanism of dopamine circuit modulation
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative Research regarding the dopaminergic system, which is highly involved in reward, voluntary motor behavior, and substance use disorders, has long focused on neurons. With evidence that microglia can modulate neuronal activity and the discovery of a novel subpopulation of microglia expressing the dopamine receptor DRD1 by our lab, support for a glial axis of dopaminergic regulation is mounting. In this proposal, I will use transgenic mouse models to investigate the determinants of microglial DRD1 expression and how it impacts the neuronal response in two instances of sustained changes in dopamine levels: juvenile dopaminergic development and chronic cocaine.

application_id: 10833492
activity_code: F31
title: Functional Role for H3 Serotonylation During Critical Periods of Postnatal Brain Development and Plasticity
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative Human neurological diseases associated with monoaminergic (e.g., serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine) dysfunction affect millions of individuals worldwide; however, treatments for these neurodevelopmental, neurodegenerative and/or psychiatric disorders remain inadequate. Here, employing a truly unique combination of biochemical, molecular, and behavioral approaches, we are examining novel, neurotransmission independent roles for serotonin in brain in the direct regulation of neuronal gene expression. The experiments in this proposal will leverage cutting-edge technologies to understand how alterations in histone serotonylation (i.e., the covalent addition of serotonin to histone proteins) directly modulate neurodevelopmental and early life neural plasticity.

title: Functional Connectivity Markers of Cognitive Network Disfunction in Chiari Malformation
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 This research aims to identify functional connectivity (FC) markers of disease for Chiari Malformation
(CM), a condition characterized by an anatomic abnormality of the cerebellar tonsils and manifests in a wide
range of often non-specific neurologic symptoms. The results will advance the fundamental understanding of
how structural abnormalities in the human brain affect FC and provide translational evidence for use of FC in

application to improve health care.

application_id: 11303020
activity_code: K23
title: HIV Clinic-based Screening for Geriatric Syndromes in Older Adults with HIV
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Older people with HIV (PWH) are at increased risk for geriatric syndromes at relatively younger ages than their
peers without HIV, but identification and management of such syndromes remains a clinical challenge in HIV
care. This proposal will use implementation research and user-centered design to implement and evaluate HIV
clinic-based screening for impaired cognition and physical function at the Montefiore Center for Positive Living
(CPL). The results from this investigation will inform best practices implementation of screening for geriatric
syndromes in PWH, linkage to appropriate specialty care, and appropriate strategies for sustaining and
expanding HIV geriatric programs in the future.

application_id: 10877158
activity_code: K23
title: Serological markers of natural immunity to Plasmodium falciparum infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Plasmodium falciparum, the most common species of malaria, kills more children than any other pathogen, and the development of a highly effective vaccine has been challenging. Children acquire immunity to malaria with repeated exposure, but the mechanism is poorly understood. We will collect samples from children who are exposed to Plasmodium-infected mosquito bites and compare the immune responses among those who develop infection to those who do not to identify possible targets for vaccines to prevent malaria infection.

application_id: 10681670
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining the universal genomic language of hallmarks in tumor development
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it represents a broad and innovative paradigm shift
in diagnosing the genetic causes of cancer, from individual “driver” mutations in 2% of the genome toward
“programs” of combinations of mutations in the entire genome. The powerful technology developed herein will
be universally applicable to the genomes of all cancer types, as well as other age-associated diseases driven by
similar genomic mechanisms. It has the long-term potential to turn genomes into powerful weapons against
several genome-driven diseases, thereby advancing NIH’s mission to reduce illness and enhance the survival
of millions of patients.

application_id: 11394749
activity_code: K23
title: Biobehavioral Reward Responses Associated with Consumption of Nutritionally Diverse Ultra-Processed Foods
org_name: ORI COMMUNITY AND EVALUATION SERVICES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The changing food environment, with increasingly abundant ultra-processed food (UPF) options, may directly
contribute to rising rates of obesity, though it is unknown which ingredients in UPF elevate their reinforcing
nature in a way that may lead to overconsumption. The proposed study is the first to systematically examine
differences in the rewarding characteristics of and physiological and metabolic responses to UPFs that are
high in fat, refined carbohydrates (like sugar), or both. Understanding the biobehavioral underpinnings that
enhance the reinforcing potential of ingredients in UPF (e.g., fat vs. refined carbohydrates) can inform novel
intervention targets for the treatment of overeating and obesity.

application_id: 10827972
activity_code: K23
title: Using Decision Making Science to Optimize Deceased Donor Kidney Allocation
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Kidney transplantation provides the best survival and quality of life for patients with kidney failure, but there is a shortage of kidneys available for transplantation in the United States. This organ shortage necessitates the use of some deceased donor kidneys with less-than-ideal characteristics, and the safe use of these kidneys requires accurate and reliable assessments of organ quality; however, the current presentation of clinical data leads to suboptimal decision making that discourages the use of transplantable marginal kidneys. This project aims to use decision making science to improve the way that this data is presented, starting with deceased donor kidney biopsy results, in order to improve the utilization of kidneys from the expanded donor and organ pool.

application_id: 11366267
activity_code: K23
title: Air Pollution Effects on Child Irritability and Brain Connectivity
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Persistent irritability in childhood impacts up to 20 percent of youth and is associated with elevated risk for
adolescent mental health problems, such as anxiety and depression, as well as significant functional
impairment, including school problems, family problems, and problems with peers. By seeking to understand
how prenatal exposure to PAH affects children’s irritability and brain function, this project is a first step towards
identifying modifiable environmental risk factors and establishing potential etiologic pathways for persistent
irritability. Such knowledge may be used in public health interventions to improve the quality of children’s
environment and support the development of novel treatment options for children with irritability.

application_id: 11308108
activity_code: K23
title: Community-based amblyopia screening using a novel device
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Amblyopia is the leading cause of vision loss in children worldwide and the majority of blind children live in low-
and middle-income countries. The current standard of care for amblyopia screening is to use devices which
detect amblyopia risk factors (ARF) with high false positive rates causing over-referral, unnecessary
examinations for children, and increased healthcare cost and resource utilization. This clinic- and community-
based study in Nepal will be the first to compare the efficacy and cost of two amblyopia screening strategies
and devices: one which detects amblyopia directly using a novel retinal birefringence scanner (RBS) device
and the other which detects ARF using an autorefractor (AR) screening device.

activity_code: F31
title: Differentiation and function of transitional monocytes in cancer metastasis to the lung
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative During the early phase of cancer metastasis to the lung, monocytes are recruited to the lung and differentiate into metastasis-associated macrophages (MAMs). Both monocytes and their progeny MAMs are known to play tumor-supporting roles and exacerbate metastasis in the lung. The goals of this proposal are to identify the signals and factors that influence monocyte differentiation and to define the function of monocytes during their transition into MAMs.

title: Symptom Care at Home-Heart Failure: Developing and piloting a symptom monitoring and self-management coaching system for patients with heart failure
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Novel therapies for the most common malignant pediatric brain tumor, medulloblastoma, are hindered by a lack of understanding of the roles played by host-derived cells at the local tumor site. application_id: 10693814
activity_code: R44
title: Remote STATE training for Insomnia in Older Adults
org_name: POSIT SCIENCE CORPORATION
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this project is to definitively evaluate the efficacy of a targeted cognitive control and alertness
computerized training program (rSTATE) designed to improve sleep regulation and brain health and extend
functional independence in healthy older adults. This intervention may benefit a large population of vulnerable
older adults that suffer from insomnia.

application_id: 11094712
activity_code: K00
title: Discovery of a cerebellar-specific BAF chromatin remodeler that is necessary for social inhibition: molecular and circuit mechanisms
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Better understanding of causative mechanisms in autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is urgently needed to inform treatment for more than 1% of children worldwide who are affected with ASD. This research identifies mutations in the neuronal BAF chromatin remodeling subunit ACTL6B as a highly penetrant cause of recessive autism and validates Actl6b knockout mice as a model. Building on these findings, the goals of the project are to uncover molecular and circuit mechanisms that cause autism-related phenotypes in Actl6b-/- mice.

title: Using miRNA to identify new therapeutic pathways for dilated cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO

activity_code: DP2
title: Optimizing the HIV Prevention and Care Continua in the United States
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Getting to zero new infections in the US will require curbing the epidemic among communities at risk of HIV acquisition. The House and Ball Community (HBC) is a population that includes many communities at risk of HIV acquisition and is underserved in the response to HIV. Research and interventions with the HBC have the potential to address and transcend geographical boundaries to reach regions most impacted by HIV. This proposal is a national mixed methods longitudinal population-based study of HIV prevention and care among the HBC. This study is the first step in a program of research that will lead to a HIV risk reduction intervention.

application_id: 10685132
activity_code: DP2
title: Cellular FM-radios: seeing, probing, and perturbing single-cell protein activity dynamics in biological systems with frequency-barcoded spatiotemporal signaling circuits
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
Many human diseases arise from dysregulation of protein activity dynamics that drive single-cell behaviors
underpinning critical multicellular functions. The proposed research will develop genetically-encoded “cellular
radio stations”: synthetic protein circuits that generate a unique frequency-modulatable (FM) barcoded signal in
the cell that can be used to unambiguously visualize, probe, and perturb single-cell protein activity dynamics in
any complex biological setting. By applying this technology to investigate oncogenic signal processing across
cancer cells and tumor organoids, we will advance our understanding of fundamental biology and identify new
approaches for the diagnosis and treatment of human disease.

application_id: 10685736
activity_code: DP2
title: Visualizing mechanisms at the intersection of chromatin, transcription, and epigenetics
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Regulatory processes at the intersection of chromatin, transcription, and chromatin are
fundamental to cellular development and growth. The mechanistic basis of gene expression
regulation, however, is not well understood. Our proposal will develop an integrated strategy to
reconstitute transcription and epigenetic processes in their native chromatin environment and
employ a novel cryogenic electron microscopy approach to improve our understanding of gene
expression regulation in health and disease and to facilitate the development of novel
therapeutics against cancers.

application_id: 10685823
activity_code: DP2
title: Under Pressure: Biophysical Mapping of Herpesvirus Capsid Assembly and Genome Packaging
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Herpesviruses infect most of the human population, establishing incurable, lifelong, dormant infections that upon
reactivation, can result in severe disease states and fatalities, particularly among the immunocompromised. The
complexity of these viruses, along with divergent aspects of viral replication among the three subfamilies infecting
humans, have hindered the identification of pan-herpesviral preventions and treatments. Therefore, the goal of
this proposal is to develop a deep understanding of how herpesvirus capsids assemble and package DNA, two
essential steps required for all herpesviruses to spread infection, and use this information to develop novel
therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 11177919
activity_code: K00
title: Interrogating the Fgl2-FcgRIIB axis: A novel mechanism mediating apoptosis of tumor-specific memory CD8+ T cells
org_name: ALLEN INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE CD8+ T cells are crucial immune cells in our body’s fight against cancer and the degree to which CD8+ T cells traffic to and infiltrate the tumor greatly impacts cancer patient survival, especially melanoma patient survival. The F99 section of this proposal focuses on a new pathway existing between CD8+ T cells and a protein present at the tumor that negatively impacts CD8+ T-cell survival. Through mouse models, we will illuminate this mechanism to be able to block this negative interaction between CD8+ T cells and this suppressive protein to increase CD8+ T-cell survival, and ultimately improve melanoma patient survival.

application_id: 11082820
activity_code: DP2
title: Sensory Augmentation, Restoration, and Modulation Using a Spinal Neuroprosthesis
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Neuroprosthetic technologies that can restore sensory perception in individuals with sensory deficits caused by
an injury or disorder are still in their infancy, while technologies that augment perception of signals outside our
perceptual world do not yet exist. This proposal aims to test a novel neuroprosthetic technology, sensory spinal
cord stimulation (SSCS), for the augmentation, restoration, and modulation of sensory perceptions in human
subjects undergoing spinal cord stimulator implantation. Successful implementation of proposed goals will launch
an innovative line of sensory augmentation research and will accelerate the development of novel methods to
treat sensory deficits caused by stroke, traumatic brain injuries, limb amputations, and spinal cord injuries.
terms: N/A
---
application_id: 11320414
activity_code: DP2
title: Base-Editing the Cancer Kinome to Enable Drug Discovery
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: NARRATIVE
Kinase proteins are druggable enzymes that regulate nearly all aspects of cell signaling in cancer cells, but
the ideal kinase targets or kinase drug combinations to kill various types of cancers have not been
established. We have developed new technologies to base edit all 556 human kinases and remove their
enzyme activity at physiologic levels. Our proposed research will decipher the functional effects of how kinase
inhibition alters cell growth, drug response, and gene expression which will accelerate kinase target validation
and enable drug discovery.
terms: N/A
---
application_id: 11375679
activity_code: DP2
title: Functional, Structural and Molecular Decoding of Astrocyte-Neuron Interaction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: Contact PD/PI: Yu, Xinzhu
PROJECT NARRATIVE
 As the most numerous non-neuronal cell types in the mammalian brain, astrocytes form close
associations with neurons and regulate various aspects of neuronal function. The goal of this project is to
elucidate the structural, functional and molecular basis mediating the communication between astrocytes and
distinct projection neurons in the prefrontal circuit. Abnormalities of astrocyte-neuron interaction have been
implicated in numerous neurological and psychiatric disorders, and thus the proposed research is highly relevant
to a broad range of mental disorders and may guide the development of innovative treatment.
Project Narrative Page 7

title: Colorado Biomedical Informatics Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: The University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus Computational Bioscience Program (CPBS) is an independent, Ph.D.-granting and postdoctoral training program based in the University of Colorado School of Medicine, with a 20-year track record of innovative and effective training of pre- and post-doctoral fellows for research careers. application_id: 11088191
activity_code: R00
title: Deep Learning Approaches to Decipher the Impact of Mobile Element Insertion on Alternative Splicing in Neurological Disorders
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Mobile element insertions (MEIs) represent a largely undefined component of the genetic architecture of neurological disorders, as a number of MEIs have been associated with alternative splicing in these disorders but large-scale genome-wide functional characterization has not been systematically performed across tissues. This program study will functionally characterize the impact of MEIs on alternative splicing from whole-genome sequencing and transcriptome sequencing in large cohorts using new developments in deep learning models. These results will enhance our understanding of the etiological role and pathogenic mechanisms associated with MEIs in neuronal development and human neurological disorders.

application_id: 11184280
activity_code: DP5
title: OCT as a Platform for Non-Invasive Virtual H&E Biopsy
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: 8. Project Narrative
We aim to develop next-generation medical imaging technologies that would allow the creation of hematoxylin
and eosin (H&E) biopsy-like images but without cutting the tissue and in real time. This imaging technology may
provide both new basic understanding of cancer development, and clinical tools to aid in early diagnosis and
tumor margin detection for better treatment management of cancer patients. As a first application, we aim to
demonstrate virtual H&E biopsy on brain tumors, ultimately giving surgeons the ability to precisely determine
brain tumor margins during surgery but before actually cutting into the brain.

application_id: 10833103
activity_code: DP1
title: MR-guided focused ultrasound to eradicate CNS viral reservoirs and promote neurogenesis in the HIV-infected brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Narrative
HIV-infected persons need to take life-long antiretroviral medications (ARVs) because these
ARVs cannot remove the hidden sources of HIV, especially those found in the brain. We aim to
use MR-guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) to safely and transiently open the blood brain-
barrier (BBB) in order to maximize the delivery of long-acting slow release ARVs, in combination
with the AAV-CRISPR-Cas9 technology to eliminate the integrated HIV in the brain. We will first
optimize the approach in rodents, and then translate it to human studies.

org_name: MAKERERE UNIVERSITY COLLEGE OF HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Africa is facing a triple burden of disease i.e. a high incidence of fatal infectious diseases, non- communicable diseases, and emerging epidemics (with pandemic potential). Research training in LICs provides an avenue to build capacity for locally generated investigations aimed at preventing and controlling these diseases. application_id: 11123595
activity_code: DP5
title: Self-Assembling Spike-EBR Nanoparticles as a Vaccine Platform Technology Against SARS-CoV-2 and Future Pandemic Coronaviruses
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: Project Narrative The COVID-19 pandemic represents the 3rd outbreak caused by zoonotic transmission of a beta-coronavirus (beta-CoV) in the last 20 years, and new vaccine strategies are urgently needed to ensure rapid global distribution, lasting protection against variants of concern (VOCs), and prevention of future CoV pandemics. The proposed research is based on the EBR nanoparticle (NP) technology that modifies membrane proteins such as the SARS-CoV-2 spike (S) protein to self-assemble into virus-resembling NPs that bud from the cell surface. The EBR technology will be applied to accomplish three goals: i) Design a hybrid mRNA vaccine that combines attributes of mRNA and protein NP vaccines by encoding the self-assembling S-EBR NP construct to enhance vaccine potency against SARS-CoV-2...

application_id: 11009687
activity_code: DP2
title: Uncovering brain-wide molecular determinants of individual memory performance across lifespan
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The ability to learn and form memories is one of the most critical functions of the nervous system and is often
disrupted in many neurological diseases; however, the set of essential memory molecules and how they
contribute to individual variation in memory performance in different physiological context, such as aging, have
yet to be defined. Here we propose to molecularly define memory and the mechanisms of memory
performance across lifespan using cutting edge technologies in an organism with a simple nervous system, C.
elegans, which can learn and remember using the same molecules as humans. By uncovering the molecules
that are necessary for memory formation, we can develop new therapies to preserve or improve memory in
diseases where this ability has been compromised.

application_id: 11129718
activity_code: R36
title: Understanding longitudinal Trajectories of Environmental Factors and Depression Among Adolescents
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: There is considerable interest in understanding factors that impact adolescent experiences of depression. We propose to conduct secondary data analysis with latent class and mixed modeling methods to holistically answer the question “How do caregiver experiences of unfair treatment, psychosocial factors, and environmental influences interact to influence adolescent depression over time?”. Findings from this study will improve our understanding of these complex dynamics, contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of the factors that impact adolescent depression, and could inform interventions and policies to support the well-being of adolescents across varied settings.

application_id: 10742373
activity_code: R21
title: Promoting Functional Neck Motion in Patients with Cerebral Palsy using a Robotic Neck Brace
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: We will study a cohort of children with CP GMFCS IV-V and characterize the movement control of their head
in sitting position. A robotic neck brace has been developed by our Robotics and Rehabilitation (ROAR) to
characterize and restore head-neck movements in subjects with neuro-motor disorders. A multi-session training
program will be designed and tested in CP children to maximize their head control.

title: Oklahoma IDeA Network of Biomedical Research Excellence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA HLTH SCIENCES CTR
phr: The Oklahoma IDeA Network of Biomedical Research Excellence (OK-INBRE) is a network of research-intensive institutions, Primary Undergraduate Institutions (PUIs), and Community Colleges (CCs) in Oklahoma that are building critical research infrastructure and providing training, research productivity, and career development of biomedical researchers and undergraduate students. OK-INBRE-funded investigators are invested in numerous projects that span cancer, infectious disease, autoimmunity, diabetes, and aging. Collectively, the OK-INBRE program supports several biomedical research topics that will further the health of Oklahomans.

application_id: 11092254
activity_code: R36
title: Examining the Impact of the Criminal Legal System on Opioid Use Disorder Treatment and Recovery: A Socioecological Perspective
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: This project highlights the urgent need to address the multi-level impact of the CLS on OUD treatment and recovery to combat the opioid epidemic among people involved with the CLS. The Substance Use and Mental Health Services Administration defines recovery as a self-directed process, and this project examines ways in which institutions and networks limit an individual’s agency in their recovery efforts. Because substance use recovery is not merely an individual process, it is important to address barriers and facilitators at all levels of impact to best effect change and have a sustained public health impact.

application_id: 11176616
activity_code: DP2
title: Immunosuppression-free transplantation through placental mimicry
org_name: ARIZONA STATE UNIVERSITY-TEMPE CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it aims to develop a method to transplant cells and
tissue in the absence of chronic systemic immunosuppression, which could lead to improved outcomes in the
general population of transplant recipients. This project is relevant to NIH’s mission, as the development of
methods to transplant cells and tissue in the absence of immunosuppression would impact tens of thousands
of transplant recipients per year.

application_id: 10988283
activity_code: K00
title: Genome Integrity Through the Cell Cycle in Homeostasis and Cancer
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Preservation of genome integrity is crucial during development, normal cellular function, and tumor suppression, and the DNA damage repair and cell division cycle pathways are at the heart of an interconnected web of molecular mechanisms. Deeper understanding of these mechanisms is required for more advanced therapeutic interventions and genetic engineering technologies. This project seeks to take on the most challenging and important questions in double-strand break repair, the cell cycle, and how myriad diverse pathways converge in the course of tumorigenesis.

application_id: 11171364
activity_code: K00
title: Improving the Generalizability of Deep Neural Networks by Teaching Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms Associated with LDCT Features
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Standard diagnostic practice for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) focuses on nodular characteristics such as diameter and growth rate over sequential scans, thus ignoring important diagnostic features in the surrounding parenchyma such as emphysema and chronic inflammation that are associated with an increased risk of a nodule being malignant. Understanding the key parenchymal and tumor-specific features together with other biologic traits that determine whether a nodule is malignant or benign will improve early diagnosis of NSCLC. We will develop convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that utilize these features and traits to provide improved diagnostic accuracy of indeterminate pulmonary nodules seen in low-dose computed tomography images.

application_id: 10905384
activity_code: R36
title: Mitochondrial Defects in 22q11.2 Deletion Using a Human-Derived Neuronal Stem Cell Model
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: 22q11.2 deletion (22qDel) is a high-risk schizophrenia-associated microdeletion. Several nuclear-encoded mitochondrial proteins are located within the 22q11.2 locus prompting the hypothesis that neuronal mitochondria may be particularly vulnerable to heterozygosity of 22q11.2 genes. This project will determine the impact of the 22q11.2 deletion on mitochondrial biology and neuronal development and may provide novel insights into its shared biology with schizophrenia.

title: Discovering T cell proteome turnover dynamics to overcome the solid tumor microenvironment
org_name: UNIV OF ARKANSAS FOR MED SCIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This study investigates T cell proteome turnover dynamics and the role it plays in tumor infiltrating T cell persistence. The identified mechanisms which endow T cells with superior adaptability will be applied to models of cellular therapy and utilized in clinical immune monitoring.

application_id: 11472309
activity_code: DP2
title: Necrotic survivors and plasma membrane integrity signaling
org_name: METHODIST HOSPITAL RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Most cell death studies are focused on how cells die, while this study focuses on the cells that
suffer from programmed necrosis but can survive. We hypothesize that the necrotic “survivors”
can stimulate oncogenesis via chemokines/cytokines secretions. We will reveal the molecular
mechanisms of the “necrotic survivors” and further target these “survivors” to improve cancer
treatments.

application_id: 11387137
activity_code: DP2
title: Identifying plasma proteomic profiles of chronic pain development in endometriosis from adolescence to adulthood
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE
Adolescents and young women with endometriosis, a gynecologic disease causing severe pelvic pain, are at an
increased risk of becoming chronic opioid users. This innovative application aims to improve and optimize pain
management for endometriosis by evaluating biomarkers in endometriosis cases diagnosed in adolescence with
follow-up data and paired blood samples collected 10 years apart from adolescence to adulthood to identify
plasma protein biomarkers of centralized, chronic pain development. Results from this study will ultimately lead to
development of novel strategies to prevent and treat chronification of pain optimized for adolescents and young
adults with endometriosis, significantly contributing to ending the current opioid pandemic.

application_id: 10917568
activity_code: DP1
title: Impact of HIV co-infections and comorbidities on circadian rhythm in the liver
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative HIV co-infection accelerates liver cirrhosis and liver cancer in chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) carriers. In addition, HIV is associated with more severe fatty liver disease. The use of unique primary cell culture models and humanized mice under conditions that maintain or disrupt the circadian rhythm of hepatocytes will determine the interaction of HIV co-infections and co-morbidities with the circadian rhythm. These studies will provide information for HIV pathogenesis in the liver and may help the development of novel antiviral strategies.

application_id: 10909934
activity_code: R01
title: Developing a walking exercise program for non-ambulatory stroke survivors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Narrative This study will examine the efficacy of a gait training program in chronic stroke survivors who cannot walk independently. We will evaluate them on their cardiovascular, pulmonary, and sensorimotor functions. The results of the proposed study may lead to the development of an effective gait training program for non- ambulatory stroke survivors who need it the most.
