application_id: 10879805
activity_code: DP2
title: Mapping polymicrobial interactions that modulate antibiotic persistence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: Project Narrative:
Antibiotic treatment failure is a major global public health threat. In addition to acquiring antibiotic resistance,
bacteria can transiently reprogram their phenotype, which bolsters their ability to survive high-dose antibiotic
treatment. This work will enhance our knowledge of how interactions with a host's microbiome impact bacterial
responses and susceptibly to different classes of antibiotics, which can potentially improve our ability to treat
recurrent infections.

title: Brain hedonic circuitry mediating 'liking' and 'wanting' for reward
org_name: MONELL CHEMICAL SENSES CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dysregulation of brain mechanisms that control hedonic function, or `liking' reactions negatively
impacts mental health, and may contribute to affective disorders.1,2 Affective neuroscience research has
identified brain hedonic hotspots in nucleus accumbens, ventral pallidum, orbitofrontal cortex, and
insula that causally amplify hedonic `liking' responses to a sweet taste, but their circuit-level
interactions remain poorly understood. The goal of the proposed studies is to verify the existence of a
newly discovered hedonic hotspot in anterior cingulate cortex, and to investigate how hedonic hotspots
interact by becoming unanimously recruited into action to generate intense `liking' reactions,
potentially leading to better therapeutic targets for affective mood disorders,...

application_id: 11015013
activity_code: K00
title: Fibrotic remodeling of lymph nodes disrupts T cell function in fibrosis and cancer
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Lymph nodes are a primary site for immune activation against malignancy, but are also a frequent site of metastasis, the leading cause of cancer related death. During cancer progression the extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffold that organizes immune cell response in the lymph node is altered, and ECM alterations such as lymph node fibrosis are associated with worse prognosis. This study focuses on investigating αvβ3 integrin as a target to reverse ECM remodeling in the lymph node during the F99 phase and whether targeting similar pathways enhance the effectiveness of immunotherapy during the K00 phase.

application_id: 11131088
activity_code: R00
title: GPRASP family as novel regulators in hematopoietic stem cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) are the self-renewing population of cells that generate all the blood lineages
throughout the life of an organism and are biomedically important as the basic units of bone marrow
transplantation, which is used to treat a myriad of malignant and non-malignant hematological diseases. This
proposal aims to characterize a family of novel regulators (GPRASPs) of HSCs and their role in HSC
transplantation, which will illuminate a new set of molecular candidates that might be targeted for improving
current therapies.

application_id: 11284739
activity_code: R36
title: Mapping How Trajectories of Perinatal Substance Use and Depression Impact Neonatal Brain Development
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The perinatal period is a critical window of plasticity and vulnerability for maternal behavior and infant
development. During this time, maternal substance use and depression are prevalent, highly comorbid, and pose
significant risks for adverse maternal and infant health outcomes; however, their co-development and combined
impact on infant brain outcomes, particularly hippocampal development, is not well understood. Understanding
how these dynamic, reciprocal, and additive factors unfold across the perinatal period will provide crucial
information for targeted screening and intervention programs that maximize this window of opportunity to improve
outcomes for mothers and infants.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Methamphetamine (METH) use most frequently occurs in the context of other substance use (polysubstance
use), but it is not currently understood how and by whom these substances are co-used, or how particular
patterns of co-use shape METH’s negative impact on public health and substance use disorder treatment

how these are differently associated with demographic, medical and mental health, and substance use
disorder treatment outcomes. Identifying sources of risk for medical and mental health comorbidities and for

application_id: 11289093
activity_code: R36
title: Decoding the Molecular Mechanisms of a Kappa Opioid Receptor Selective Negative Allosteric Modulator
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative
Antagonists of kappa opioid receptors have demonstrated potential for treatments ranging from substance use disorder to
treatment-resistant depression. However, their complex signaling and pharmacology has limited their clinical utility,
making a deeper understanding of the structural and functional landscape crucial for developing therapeutics with
optimized efficacy and potency. This work aims to investigate the relationship between various structural states and the
pharmacological profile of a newly discovered κOR-selective negative allosteric modulator, to reveal insights that could
lead to improved therapies for κOR-related disorders and across the entire opioid receptor family.

title: Stability and Change in Psychosocial Risk and Protective Profiles for Substance Use in Adolescents With and Without ADHD: A Person-Centered Approach
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Adolescents with ADHD are at higher risk for early substance use, particularly alcohol, cannabis, and nicotine,
often leading to poorer substance use outcomes compared to peers without ADHD. Psychosocial factors
across peer, family, and school domains are thought to contribute to this vulnerability, but little research has
used person-centered methods to examine the unique unfolding of these risk and protective factors across
adolescence and the impact of this on substance use patterns of youth with and without ADHD. Understanding
individual differences in combinations of and changes in ecological risk and protective factors for substance
use over time for youth with and without ADHD may provide crucial context for identifying youth who are
particularly vulnerable to poor...

application_id: 11057011
activity_code: R38
title: UC Davis StARR Training Program in Veterinary and Human Health
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The UC Davis Stimulating Access to Research in Residency (StARR) is a unique StARR program as it
incorporates research training for medicine and veterinary medicine residents during their critical formative
academic periods. The UCD StARR program will be an institutional resource providing structured research
training pathways that align with the needs of individual trainees, while addressing the need to grow academic
clinician-scientists and a translational science workforce. The program takes advantage of rich mentoring
interactions between the UCD Schools of Medicine and Veterinary Medicine that will mentor two MD/DO and
two DVM postdoctoral residents with a goal of enabling the next generation of transdisciplinary clinician-
scientists.

title: Reducing racial disparities in AD/ADRD: Addressing structural discrimination and resilience
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: The long-term vision of this LEADR proposal is to erase racial disparities in Alzheimer’s disease/Alzheimer’s disease related dementias (AD/ADRD) prevalence and burden. application_id: 10851858
activity_code: DP1
title: Targeting the host metabolome to reverse drug-induced epigenetic changes
org_name: WAKE FOREST UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Appropriate regulation of chromatin structure and gene expression is critical for normal synaptic plasticity, and this regulation is markedly disrupted in pathological substance use disorders. Our laboratory has found that metabolites that act as key cofactors for chromatin modifying enzymes are markedly altered by drugs of abuse. These studies will identify mechanistically how metabolites and their interactions with chromatin modifying enzymes affect brain and behavior and will provide strategies for targeting the metabolome to reduce pathological drug use.

application_id: 10906354
activity_code: F31
title: Neural Processing of Native and Prosthetic Vestibular Signals for Postural Control
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The vestibular system located in the inner ear senses the motion of the head, and vestibular loss is a debilitating condition that leads to problems with vision, gait, and balance. A notable recent development is the multichannel vestibular prosthesis, a neural implant that aims to replace the function of damaged vestibular peripheral organs. In order to test the efficacy of the device and understand how the brain interprets vestibular signals during balance, this research aims to establish an animal model of balance control and examine the underlying neural mechanisms of the vestibular contribution to balance.

application_id: 10914672
activity_code: K01
title: Shared Decision-Making to Reduce Racial Disparities in Oral Anticoagulation Use in Patients with Non-Valvular Atrial Fibrillation
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Racial disparities in the use of OAC in patients with NVAF are notable given the increased risk of stroke and death in Blacks with NVAF. Shared decision making is guideline indicated by cardiovascular professional organizations for discussions about the risk and benefits of OAC, but it is less likely to occur in Blacks with NVAF. This research will identify barriers and facilitators to shared decision making, develop a patient decision support tool to assist in facilitating shared decision making with the goal of improving decision quality, increasing OAC use among Blacks and decreasing disparities in OAC use between Blacks and Whites.

title: Examining the genetic regulation of A-to-I editing and mediation of Alzheimer's disease
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a highly heritable disease characterized by progressive cognitive decline, and large-scale genetic approaches have been adopted to study the etiology of the disease. This proposal will harmonize genomic and transcriptomic data to understand how genetic variants alter adenosine-to-inosine (A- to-I) RNA editing, a post-transcriptional modification observably dysregulated in AD patient tissues. Understanding how AD-associated genetic variants modulate pre- and post-transcriptional processes will illuminate genes and pathways which can be therapeutically targeted to restore cellular function, or considered as biomarkers to support early detection of AD.

title: Investigating physiological pathways modulating tau homeostasis: potential implications for Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementia
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Tau abnormalities are causal for neurodegeneration in Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) and AD-related dementias (ADRDs), but how they arise is unclear. Here, we will test the hypothesis that the six-transmembrane GPI-anchor cleaving enzyme, GDE2, regulates GPI-anchored mediated pathways to regulate post-translational modifications of tau that are relevant to disease. application_id: 10997096
activity_code: F31
title: Decision-making consequences of decreased memory precision with age
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE By retrieving past experiences from memory, humans and other animals can infer the value of choice options, including ones they have never encountered before. In this proposal, we aim to advance our understanding of how memory contributes to value-based choice by highlighting its role in shaping how and whether value is generalized across exemplars of a similar kind. Specifically, we suggest that the precision with which the past is remembered modulates our tendency to generalize, and propose to highlight the behavioral, neural, and computational signatures of this process by focusing on aging, which is known to affect memory precision.

activity_code: F31
title: Combining Chemical Biology and Machine Learning to Generate Reproducible Amyloid Fibrils
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Aging-related disorders, including Parkinson's Disease, Alzheimer's Disease and related dementias (ADRDs), affect over 8 million people in the US, and present a significant challenge to traditional, structure0based drug discovery methods due to the shape shifting nature of the aggregating proteins in these diseases. The proposed research aims to develop a new assay for determining fibril morphologies (FMs) in these diseases, addressing the variability in current in vitro assays critical for understanding compound binding and cellular toxicity. This advancement will facilitate the creation of a comprehensive FM library, enhancing reproducibility and accelerating progress in ADRD research through high-throughput screening and machine learning techniques.

title: Identifying Global Rejuvenation Mechanisms in Tissues that Reverses Age-Related Phenotypes in Planarians
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE During aging, the progression of organ frailty and diseases is often linked to the dysregulation of tissue homeostasis, which can be attributed to changes in the microenvironmental niches that regulate, maintain, and alter adult stem cell function. Utilizing Ex-Scope, an ultra-high resolution spatial transcriptomics technology I recently developed, I aim to characterize how aging and regeneration in planarians alter cell types and their corresponding expression (mRNA) in microenvironmental niches. title: Characterizing a Serine-Threonine Phosphatase Essential for Asexual Replication in Plasmodium falciparum
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative The parasite which causes malaria uses a unique, specialized structure known as the basal complex to separate daughter cells during division. We identified a serine-threonine phosphatase involved in the basal complex which is essential for parasite growth and replication. Our goal is to identify and characterize this protein and its substrates and other interactants to determine the role it plays during the division process, as it could be an effective target for developing new anti-malarial drugs.

application_id: 10770984
activity_code: F31
title: Identification of Trichomonas vaginalis resistance targets to inform future drug development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Trichomonas vaginalis, the most common non-viral sexually transmitted infection worldwide, disproportionately affects African Americans and is associated with increased risk of HIV acquisition, adverse birth outcomes, infertility, and other adverse reproductive morbidities. Metronidazole is used for the treatment of trichomoniasis, but in up 10% of cases, T. vaginalis isolates are resistant. Results from the proposed studies will provide new knowledge about metronidazole resistance in T. vaginalis that will inform development of future assays for rapid metronidazole-resistance detection as well as novel drugs.

title: Investigating genetic regulators of Mycobacterium tuberculosis cell division and their role in persistent tuberculosis infection
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) is an exceptionally successful human pathogen largely due to its unique mechanism of replication and the bacteria’s ability to slow or halt growth to promote its survival in vivo. title: Defining mechanisms of heterogeneity in cell fate decisions during Yersinia pseudotuberculosis infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: These studies will define the molecular interactions and bacterial factors regulating apoptotic and pyroptotic single-cell death outcomes during Yersinia infection, informing potential immunological therapies to better combat bacterial infections. application_id: 10804589
activity_code: F31
title: Dual antiretroviral loaded nanoparticles for long-acting suppressive HIV therapy
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative Viral persistence due to low antiretroviral (ARV) penetration in secondary lymphoid tissues (SLTs) and poor adherence to daily ARV regimen remain major barriers to achieving a functional cure for HIV. We want to develop “stealth” long-acting lipid polymeric nanoparticles (GM3-PLA-NPs) that can potentially increase ARV entry and retention in SLTs by forming cellular drug depots in CD169+ macrophages. Our proposed studies will investigate the stealth and biodistribution mechanisms of GM3-PLA-NPs to elucidate new strategies for targeted delivery of safe long-acting drugs in the SLTs and hence aid in the development of nanotherapeutics for prevention and treatment of HIV.

title: Investigating differentiation, maintenance, and reactivation of the Toxoplasma chronic stage
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: 8. PROJECT NARRATIVE Toxoplasma gondii chronically infects one quarter of the world’s population, and its reactivation can cause debilitating and life-threatening disease. Key to T. gondii’s pathogenesis is its ability to enter a reversible, semi- quiescent chronic stage that is resistant to clearance by the host immune response and all existing pharmacotherapies. This proposal will investigate how the T. gondii chronic-stage developmental program is initiated and, later, overturned during reactivation, providing new molecular insights to inform the design of novel therapies for toxoplasmosis directed at a radical cure.

title: Evaluating the Influence of an Improved Piped Water Network on Water Quality and Gut Microbiome Composition Among Children in Mozambique
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The gut microbiome, which begins to develop in early life, is associated with many important physiological functions. Harmful exposures during the first year of life (e.g., heavy metals and enteropathogen exposures that are prevalent in drinking water in low-income contexts) can cause gut dysbiosis, which is associated with many adverse health outcomes. This project will investigate if and how improvements to drinking water quality and access influence infant gut microbiome composition among children in Beira, Mozambique, which is critical to improve our understanding of the association between drinking water quality and access on both acute and chronic sequelae related to gut dysbiosis.

application_id: 10974027
activity_code: F31
title: Deciphering the roles of RIFIN and STEVOR parasite antigens in severe malaria pathogenesis via transcriptomics and immune profiling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Malaria kills hundreds of thousands of children in sub-Saharan Africa each year, but it remains unclear why some infections progress from mild to severe illness. Laboratory studies suggest that diverse parasite antigens called RIFINs and STEVORs may have several roles in severe malaria pathogenesis, but they have not been studied in clinical infections. The goal of this proposal is to identify RIFIN and STEVOR subtypes that are associated with vulnerability to severe malaria in sub-Saharan African children, illuminating the importance of these proteins in pathogenesis and informing the development of vaccines and therapeutics.

title: Renal and Electrolyte Disease and Hypertension

phr: Project Narrative Kidney disease and hypertension affect more than 30 million and 70 million adults in the United States, respectively. The Division of Renal Diseases and Hypertension at the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus T32 Training program provides state-of-the-art research training for MD and PhD scientists in both laboratory-based and clinical studies, with the goal of training the next generation of leaders in the field of nephrology research.

application_id: 10918088
activity_code: DP5
title: OCT as a Platform for Non-Invasive Virtual H&E Biopsy
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: 8. Project Narrative We aim to develop next-generation medical imaging technologies that would allow the creation of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) biopsy-like images but without cutting the tissue and in real time. This imaging technology may provide both new basic understanding of cancer development, and clinical tools to aid in early diagnosis and tumor margin detection for better treatment management of cancer patients. As a first application, we aim to demonstrate virtual H&E biopsy on brain tumors, ultimately giving surgeons the ability to precisely determine brain tumor margins during surgery but before actually cutting into the brain.

application_id: 11135567
activity_code: R00
title: Controlling renal oxidative stress in CKD via targeting FGF23 bioactivity
org_name: JACKSON LABORATORY
phr: Project Narrative
Chronic kidney disease (CKD), which affects 37 million Americans, progresses with the loss of important proteins
such as Klotho, leading to severe endocrine disease, as well as increased renal oxidative stress that further
damages the kidneys. In this career development proposal, I will enhance my computational skills to identify how
the interactions of Klotho and its ligand fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23) protect against oxidative stress at
the molecular level. These studies will provide the backdrop for key career development as well as help to identify
new pathways regulated by Klotho and FGF23 that may slow CKD progression, and potentially uncover novel
therapeutic targets.

application_id: 11137642
activity_code: DP2
title: Exploring metabolic governance of immune cell form and function
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tissue-resident macrophages (TRMs) are vital executives of tissue homeostasis and play
important roles in tumors, yet the (metabolic) pathways important for their development, tissue-
specific identity, and function are not well understood. Here, we put forward a framework to
identify and characterize the metabolic programs overseeing these key aspects of TRM biology,
including innovative approaches to explore macrophage metabolism within human tissues.
Through these studies, we will obtain a deeper understanding of TRMs across health and disease,
and ascertain novel metabolic vulnerabilities that can be exploited for immunomodulation of these
essential cells.

application_id: 11131242
activity_code: R36
title: An Analysis Of The Multi-Level Factors That Impact Provision Of Emergency Medical Services To Hispanic Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Emergency medical services (EMS) serve as an important entry point in the United States’(U.S.) healthcare
system, especially for Hispanic older adults who have challenges accessing preventive and diagnostic care, and
who rely on emergency services to meet their healthcare needs. The goal of this dissertation is to advance our
understanding of the individual, neighborhood, and structural-level factors that impact the provision of EMS to
Hispanic older adults in the prehospital setting. The study findings can inform efforts to make EMS more
accessible, age-friendly, and equitable.

title: The Effects of Structural Inequities and Syndemics on Willingness to Participate in HIV Research among Black Women
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Black women with BW face sociostructural inequities including structural racism, HIV stigma, and medical
mistrust in addition to the syndemic effects of substance use, intimate partner violence and adverse mental
health, which act as barriers to health seeing behaviors that contribute to poor HIV outcomes. Additionally,
BW have been historically under enrolled in HIV clinical research that does not contend with these historical
circumstances. As a first-generation, Black female predoctoral student, this explanatory sequential mixed
methods R36 dissertation proposal is the first to attempt to quantify and contextualize the impacts of
sociostructural inequities and syndemics on BW’s participation in HIV research to inform multilevel

org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Experiences with early childhood adversity can disrupt the typical development of a child and are associated
with unfavorable educational results, such as low standardized reading scores. With over half of the United
States population reporting at least one adverse event in their lifetime, this study examines how adverse
experiences affect several cognitive processes that facilitate planning actions, solving problems, and staying
engaged on reading tasks among children exposed to adversity. By identifying the specific cognitive processes
influenced by adversity, this study aims to provide valuable insights into how negative life experiences affect a
consequential educational outcome (i.e., reading) that has important lifelong implications to inform future
research, theory...

title: Training the Long-Term Services and Supports Dementia Care Workforce in Provision of Care to Sexual and Gender Minority Residents
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Narrative: This study is designed to help improve the care of SGM with AD/ADRD in LTSS by conducting a rigorous study of the policies of LTSS agencies in Minnesota and by training the state’s LTSS workforce in SGM culturally responsive care. activity_code: DP2
title: Circulatronics: A New Paradigm for Biomedical Implants
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Bioelectronic implants provide powerful tools for healthcare as well as basic research but their implantation require invasive surgery which is associated with pain and adverse physical as well as psychological effects. We are developing a technology in which ultra-miniaturized bioelectronic devices can get implanted in target regions and provide localized sensing and treatment without the need for surgery. This administrative supplement will extend our technology to Alzheimer’s disease which can lead to a novel therapeutics for preventing the spread and even development of this disease.

application_id: 11134805
activity_code: DP2
title: Determining Age-Dependent Metabolic Changes in Tumors and Their Microenvironment
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
 Although both cancer rates and metabolic deregulation increase with age, it is not well understood how
age-dependent metabolic changes affect cancer initiation and progression. In addition, we have limited
knowledge of how age-associated metabolic differences in the tumor microenvironment contribute to
carcinogenesis and how these changes are affected by environmental factors such as diet and microbial
metabolites. In this proposal, we plan to elucidate age- associated metabolic changes in tumors and their
microenvironment using advanced single cell technologies, which may lead to discovering metabolic liabilities
and improve existing therapies.

application_id: 11192386
activity_code: R00
title: The Development of Youth Resilience to Neighborhood Disadvantage
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: Project Narrative
Neighborhood disadvantage (e.g., poverty and exposure to violence) is a robust indicator of youth adjustment
difficulties across most domains of development, yet there are youth who thrive despite significant
neighborhood adversity. This project investigates: (1) how resilience changes as youth reach important
developmental milestones and freely explore their environments, (2) whether these developmental changes
differ by resilience domain (psychiatric, social, emotional, academic), and (3) how warm and involved parenting
as well as neighborhood protective factors might shape their development. In doing so, the proposed project
can identify specific and modifiable protective factors that promote resilience to chronic neighborhood risk.

application_id: 10949086
activity_code: K00
title: Myeloid cell-expressed PTPN22 and anti-tumor immunity
org_name: NEW YORK GENOME CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in key immune regulatory molecules are associated with autoimmunity; accordingly, targeting of these molecules pharmacologically have resulted in revolutionized cancer treatment in the shape of immune checkpoint immunotherapies. Thus, through the F99 phase I will characterize how loss of the autoimmune-related molecule PTPN22 in myeloid cells drives an augmented immune response against tumors. Subsequently, through the K00 phase I will identify novel autoimmune related SNPs correlating with tumor immune infiltration and characterize them in engineered conditional knockout mice to understand their contribution to tumor progression and immunotherapy efficacy.

application_id: 11146849
activity_code: R33
title: Integrative Training Program for Pediatric Sickle Cell Pain
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chronic pain significantly diminishes all aspects of quality of life among children and adolescents with sickle cell
disease (SCD), yet there are limited effective treatments available to date. This research will adapt and tailor an
existing evidence-based innovative mind-body, cognitive-behavioral, and neuromuscular movement intervention
to meet the unique needs of adolescents with chronic sickle cell pain and test the new intervention (termed I-
STRONG for SCD) in a multi-center randomized clinical trial. The long-term goal of this research is to reduce
functional impairment, pain, and common psychological comorbidities such as depression and anxiety, among
youth with chronic pain in SCD to mitigate disparities in pain and health outcomes.

application_id: 11166720
activity_code: U54
title: Career Enhancement Core
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE 
SCORE-X CEC will provide education, training, and tools for ground-breaking research that integrates basic, molecular, clinical, and translational research, including “big data” analysis, systems immunology, genetics, and artificial intelligence (AI) in sex-differences in autoimmunity. The CEC will collaborate and coordinate with numerous established Stanford programs to increase the participation of trainees from varied backgrounds, including high school, undergraduate, medical, and graduate students, as well as postgraduate trainees and newly independent faculty.

application_id: 11172191
activity_code: U54
title: Rare Organic Acidemias Research (ROAR) Consortium
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: OVERALL PROJECT NARRATIVE
The ROAR consortium is the first, multi-site, prospective longitudinal U.S. study devoted solely to the study of
organic acidemias. The data from this consortium will lead to a greater understanding of the natural history of
organic acidemias. In addition, the results will contribute to evidence-based management and facilitate clinical
trial readiness for new therapies for organic acidemias.

application_id: 11169554
activity_code: U54
title: Career Enhancement Core
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MISSISSIPPI MED CTR
phr: Career Enhancement Core (CEC) 
Narrative 
The overall goal of the Career Enhancement Core of the Mississippi Specialized Center of Research Excellence on Sex Differences is to develop promising junior faculty into independent investigators by providing a stimulating and productive mentoring environment, provide research assistance and training in performance of sex differences and women's health research, and foster career development through training application and support. Research into mechanisms responsible for sex differences in cardiovascular, renal, and metabolic diseases are imperative to find new therapeutic options to improve overall human health, and reduce health care burdens, especially in areas with high cardiovascular disease prevalence, high poverty, low medical care acces...

application_id: 10983703
activity_code: UE5
title: Pacific Computational Genomics and Data Sciences Research Education Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF HAWAII AT MANOA
phr: Project Narrative
This program is aimed to develop, implement and share undergraduate- and Master’s-level
curricula and methods in computational genomics and data science (CGDS) education and
research, and interact with the community scientists and members of CGDS Educational Hub.
The project will create CGDS curricula with a focus on the utility of NIH cloud resources for
biomedical research and education. This program will serve the diverse and underrepresented
students and scientific community members to improve knowledge and applications of CGDS.

title: Evaluating a National Person-Centered Training Program to Strengthen the Dementia Care Workforce

phr: Project Narrative
This project will compare two training models of an evidence-based online dementia care training program for

activity_code: DP2
title: A novel framework for quantifying metabolic brain health
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Metabolic dysfunction plays a crucial role in disease progression and response to treatment. There are currently
very few methods that can non-invasively and repeatedly image metabolism and, as a result, metabolic
information is largely absent in clinical decision making. To improve our understanding of the role of metabolism
in disease, injury, and recovery, we will develop a new approach for medical imaging termed
“metaboloradiomics.”

application_id: 10762439
activity_code: K00
title: Cortical Inhibition and Mobility in Older Adults
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Nearly a quarter of all Americans age 65+ report serious mobility problems, and mobility problems predict greater risk of dementia; thus, preserving mobility in older age is an NIA priority. Our past work suggests that the cortical inhibitory system contributes to motor declines with aging. This proposal will test if cortical inhibition relates to individual differences in walking and balance in older age (F99) and mild cognitive impairment (K00), and whether aerobic exercise affects cortical inhibition (K00). The findings from this work could lead to the development of novel interventions to preserve mobility and prevent disability in aging and mild cognitive impairment.

application_id: 10908879
activity_code: DP2
title: Unraveling Fundamental Mechanisms of Interorgan Crosstalk in Osteoarthritis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Our work, indicating that fat is a source of systemic inflammation contributing to the loss of cartilage in articular
joints, has shifted the historical paradigm that osteoarthritis is a wear and tear disease. We posit that the
complex interorgan crosstalk between fat and musculoskeletal tissues is key to unlocking a mechanistic
understanding of disease progression and pain. The goal of this proposal is to define whether cell signaling,
cell migration, or the nervous system are facilitating fat-joint crosstalk to enable the development of first-in-
class OA therapeutics targeting the multi-organ interface to improve whole-body health.

application_id: 10910697
activity_code: DP2
title: Uncovering cellular and molecular adaptations for whole-organism rejuvenation
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Aging is driven by accumulated damage in cells over time that disrupts normal tissue function. Often,
this process is exacerbated by repeated injuries that stimulate proliferation and wound-induced
inflammation. This proposal leverages a highly aging-resistant organism that reproduces solely through
asexual reproduction and whole-body regeneration to uncover the cellular and molecular basis for
whole-organism rejuvenation and anti-aging.

application_id: 11062753
activity_code: DP2
title: Shifting Suicide Prevention Paradigms: Looking Beyond Clinical Settings
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative: Shifting Suicide Prevention Paradigms: Looking Beyond Clinical Settings
The goal of this research is to shift the paradigm of suicide prevention research by prioritizing social
determinants through broad, but strategic, partnerships with industries outside of mental health care. By
transforming psychological autopsy methodology into social autopsy and surveying and interviewing individuals
who work in industries dealing with the depths of life disruption (i.e., family law, mortgage foreclosure, and
unemployment processing), this project challenges the status quo of suicide prevention research that is
anchored in clinical environments. This unconventional path through non-mental health industries is the
chance to bring new perspectives and novel ideas for better, richer, ...

phr: Project Narrative This renewal application for the Integrative Liver Cell Core (ILCC) proposes its continuation of unique and specialized services involving isolation and analysis of different liver cell types. application_id: 10840430
activity_code: K00
title: Targeting Endogenous Retrovirus Regulation for Augmenting Cancer Immunotherapy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Immunotherapies have been revolutionary for treating a variety of cancer types, but still many patients do not respond or become resistant to currently available therapies, highlighting the need for more effective melanoma treatments. Endogenous retroelement re-expression may be a strategy for rendering tumors more immunogenic and therefore more treatable through immunotherapies, but this remains understudied. This proposal will examine the role of factors controlling expression of ERVs in augmenting the anti-tumor immune response, which will improve our understanding of cancer immunology and may identify new targets for therapeutic development.

application_id: 11077319
activity_code: K00
title: Elucidating the role of endocrine aging as a risk factor for Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Women are disproportionately affected by Alzheimer’s disease (AD), yet the degree to which female reproductive aging shapes the structural and functional architecture of the human brain is severely understudied. During the menopausal transition, ovarian production of estradiol and progesterone declines by up to 90%, and these sex steroid hormones play neuroprotective roles in the brain to support the structure and function of regions vulnerable to neurodegeneration. The proposed studies will use state-of-the-art brain imaging techniques to establish the endocrine basis of neural aging in midlife women and provide critical insight into whether the brain demonstrates vulnerability to AD pathology as a function of the menopausal transition.

activity_code: K00
title: Evaluating the role of cytomegalovirus and age on lung immune homeostaasis and response to respiratory infections
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The immune homeostatic state influences immune responses to infection, but is itself is a function of age, environmental stressors, and underlying genetics. Therefore, it is critical to understand the interplay between these factors and their overall impact on systemic and organ specific immunity at the steady state. This proposal studies the mechanism by which genetic variants in Methyl-CpG binding domain protein 1 regulates steady state antibody levels, and further proposes to extend the work on immune homeostasis to investigate the interaction between age and cytomegalovirus in modifying the immune architecture in the lungs and responses to respiratory infections.

title: Neurovascular Contributions to Cognitive and Mental health in Older Adult Critical Care Survivors
org_name: SPAULDING REHABILITATION HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative: Older adults are more prone to experiencing poorer long-term physical, mental, and cognitive health due to age-related frailty, which can become exacerbated during episodes of critical illness that results in admission to an Intensive Care Unit (ICU). Prior reports suggest that the cognitive dysfunction is related to physical health but mechanistic links between skeletal muscle and brain have been under explored. Therefore, the goal of this project is to determine the associations between elevated neurotoxic kynurenines with physical and cognitive in older patients in the ICU and after discharge.

title: The mechanical role of the glycocalyx in cancer cell adhesion
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Many types of cancer cell utilize a special outer layer – called a glycocalyx – to help stick to and invade soft tissues such as the brain, lung, and breast. This proposal seeks to 1) use DNA-based nanosensors to elucidate how the glycocalyx helps cancer cells become invasive, and 2) develop screening methods for early detection of glycocalyx-coated cancer cells in the bloodstream. phr: Project Narrative TANK-binding kinase 1 (TBK1), a critical downstream effector of mutant active KRAS, is central to Axl- driven epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in KRAS-mutant Pancreatic Cancer (PDA); however, the mechanism of how TBK1 drives EMT has yet to be elucidated. application_id: 11229906
activity_code: K00
title: Deciphering molecular mechanisms of Epithelial Plasticity
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Despite significant research efforts, the function of EMT in metastasis has yet to be fully elucidated and metastatic cancers and relapse remain the primary cause of cancer related deaths. Using a novel patient- derived organoid lineage tracing system, live-cell imaging, and single cell analyses, I aim to investigate the contribution of EMT to metastasis in metastatic renal cell carcinoma as well as mechanisms underlying epithelial plasticity. Understanding this process further will aid in the development of effective metastatic cancer therapies and will direct future research directions in metastasis research.

application_id: 11141163
activity_code: K00
title: Translation of small open reading frames in 3'UTR enhances translation of canonical open reading frames
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Novel mechanisms for post-transcriptional gene expression regulation continue to emerge. I recently identified the role of ribosome to enhance translation of canonical open reading frames by translating small ORFs (downstream ORFs or dORFs) in the 3’UTR. Since dORF is a novel, widespread and strong regulatory element among vertebrates, this proposal aims to study the mechanism of dORF regulation and the biological impact of dORF dysregulation in cancer. This will help us better understand post-transcriptional regulation in cancer and further, may provide dORF as new diagnostic regions of the mRNA and potential targets for drug design.

title: Characterizing Chromatin Remodeling Mechanisms of Chemo-Sensitivity and Resistance in Pediatric Solid Tumors
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chromatin remodeling complexes are dysregulated in more than 20% of human malignancies, including adult and pediatric cancers. In this study, we will leverage the low somatic mutation frequencies in rhabdoid tumor and alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma to mechanistically interrogate how chromatin remodeling drives both therapeutic sensitivity and resistance. We will use the information to identify novel therapeutic vulnerabilities that increase the penetrance of therapeutic response and restore chemotherapeutic sensitivity.

activity_code: K00
title: A Multi-omics Approach to Examine Symptoms and Medication Adherence in Women with Breast Cancer
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: A substantial proportion of women are not adherent to aromatase inhibitor (AI) therapy, primarily due to their experience of highly variable treatment- and/or disease- related symptoms. Symptoms and adherence, especially their impact on each other, have not been well-characterized temporally, nor have potential biologic mechanisms related to AI absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination (ADME). The purpose of the proposed F99/K00 training and research will utilize a biobehavioral, multi-omics approach to gain a deeper understanding of the temporal variability and interplay of symptoms, adherence, and omics to guide future interventions and their timing in women with breast cancer.

application_id: 10818666
activity_code: K00
title: Fibrotic remodeling of lymph nodes disrupts T cell function in fibrosis and cancer
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Lymph nodes are a primary site for immune activation against malignancy, but are also a frequent site of metastasis, the leading cause of cancer related death. During cancer progression the extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffold that organizes immune cell response in the lymph node is altered, and ECM alterations such as lymph node fibrosis are associated with worse prognosis. This study focuses on investigating αvβ3 integrin as a target to reverse ECM remodeling in the lymph node during the F99 phase and whether targeting similar pathways enhance the effectiveness of immunotherapy during the K00 phase.

application_id: 11093824
activity_code: K00
title: Characterizing CAFs in Metastasis as a Function of Breast Cancer Cell Phenotype
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative After metastasis to the lungs, breast cancer cells are exposed to unique forces and matrix proteins that are not present at the primary tumor. My previous findings indicate that cyclic stretching, mimicking breathing dynamics within a 3D model of the early metastatic niche, can drive tumor cells into a state of growth arrest without widespread cell death. Because stromal cells are the key contributor of matrix composition and architecture, my goal is to evaluate the unique fibroblast subtypes conditioned by breast cancer cells at different stages of the metastatic cascade and characterize how the tissue changes they produce alter the metastatic progression of disseminated breast cancer cells.

org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will combine computational modeling and neuroimaging methods to study social and cognitive computations performed by the posterior cerebellum. The F99 phase will examine how the posterior cerebellum uniquely contributes to monetary and social reward processing, improving our basic scientific understanding of the cerebellum's core algorithmic capabilities and revealing the cerebellum's potential range of neural computations. The K00 phase will explore the cerebellum's mechanistic role in autism spectrum disorder through a reinforcement learning framework, providing insight into how a disruption in these processes may cause the canonical social deficits observed in this disorder.

application_id: 11170232
activity_code: DP2
title: Optogenetic engineering of thermogenic adipose tissue as a novel cell therapy for obesity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Obesity and the metabolic syndrome arise in the setting of chronic energy surplus, and there are no current
treatment modalities designed to augment energy expenditure. Thermogenic adipose tissue represents a
promising anti-obesity target that consumes energy to produce heat, and is well suited to ex vivo expansion
and genetic engineering. This project aims to use peripheral optogenetic stimulation of thermogenic adipose
tissue to augment energy expenditure and provide a viable therapeutic option for the lifelong maintenance of
weight loss.

title: Basic neural processing mechanisms of live human face viewing
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Face perception is central to social functioning, with dysfunction being common to a range of neuropsychiatric disorders. Based on previous research showing that visual sensing behavior modulates face processing neural activity, we are proposing to use dynamic causal modelling to assess processing during a dynamic emotional expression viewing task in those with varying degrees of psychosis, in order to test a theory that a visual processing neural network modulated by eye behavior emerges during face viewing, and is dysfunctional in those with psychiatric illness. application_id: 11074963
activity_code: K00
title: Evaluating Changes to the Metabolic Profile of the Central Nervous System due to Active NeuroHIV Infection
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The mammalian brain is energetically demanding with a unique lipid profile, and fatty acid dysregulation in the brain is associated with neurologic disorders including depression and autism spectrum disorders. Specific aspects of fatty acid metabolism such as bioenergetics and lipid homeostasis that may render the brain vulnerable to disease when perturbed are unknown, and studying brain bioenergetic capacity for beta-oxidation and transcriptional responses to lipid availability is fundamental to understanding how brain metabolism impacts nervous system structure, function, cognition, and predisposition to neuropathology. Understanding the metabolic contexts that predispose the brain to duress and impairment may direct future neurological interventions.

application_id: 11009690
activity_code: DP2
title: Chemical Control of Misfolded Protein Fate
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: 8. Project Narrative
Over 6.4 million Americans are living with a neurodegenerative disorder for which there are no
disease-modifying therapies available. The goal of this application is to develop generalizable
approaches for the study and treatment of neurodegenerative disorders by discovering new ways
to target the misfolded proteins that are a hallmark of this class of diseases with small molecules.
If successful, this research will enable scientists to address the long-standing mechanistic
disconnect between pathology and phenotype in proteinopathies, and accelerate discovery of
therapeutics.

application_id: 10912811
activity_code: K00
title: Optimizing adherence to lung cancer screening: Applying theory and implementation science to participant engagement
org_name: HACKENSACK UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Lung cancer screening efficacy is highly dependent on adherence to recommended guidelines, with annual screening indicated for approximately 90% of all individuals who receive baseline scans. Initial reports of real- world screening implementation show adherence to annual screening guidelines are less than 50%, demonstrating a critical need for improvement to optimize the individual and population health benefits of lung cancer screening. I aim to address the urgent need for low-cost, feasible, and effective interventions focused on facilitating lung cancer screening adherence by developing and implementing a set of clear and engaging reminder messages that target and tailor information specific to lung cancer screening participants.

the dopamine D4 receptor (D4R) and its variants, the more common D4.4R variant, and the ADHD- and

application_id: 11062734
activity_code: DP2
title: High-throughput methods for measuring cortical synaptic connectivity at single-cell resolution
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: Project Narrative
 The brain is composed of diverse sub-types of neurons who communicate via specific patterns
of synaptic connectivity. This connectivity is responsible for normal brain function and is thought to be
altered in psychiatric disorders, but we lack methods to reliably measure the cell-type specific patterns
of synaptic connectivity. I propose to develop high-throughput methods for measuring the circuit
connectivity using viral, functional, and molecular approaches. These methods will be used to identify
circuit changes that underlie psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder.

title: A Highly Precise Platform Technology for Controlling Diverse Clinical Targets in Addiction
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Addiction is a debilitating disorder that affects ~20 million people and incurs an annual economic burden of $740
billion in the United States alone. Unfortunately, intensive drug discovery efforts have largely failed to stem the
rising toll of addiction, underscoring the need for a better understanding of this disease and a paradigm-shift in

title: Chemical biology tools for illuminating cannabinoid signaling pathways in opioid use disorder.
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The recent surge in the use of the highly-potent synthetic opioid fentanyl has exacerbated the opioid epidemic
and underscores a significant public health crisis. A potential therapeutic target for opioid use disorder lies in
cannabinoid receptors, which are co-expressed with opiate receptors in the brain's reward centers and
modulate opiate-associated behaviors. This proposal aims to use chemical biology to investigate cannabinoid-
opiate interactions, which will pave the way for new therapeutic strategies in the fight against the intensifying
opioid crisis.

application_id: 11168791
activity_code: DP1
title: Individual differences through self-reinforcement of suboptimal strategies
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Understanding the origin of individual differences in behavior is not only a question of deep fundamental
interest, but is also central to understanding the variable penetrance of neuropsychiatric disease. We propose
and intend to test a new hypothesis regarding the origin of individual differences: that they arise in part from
small differences in initial conditions caused to be amplified by positive feedback, such that different individuals
from different reward associations within the same environment. This results from the fact that the world is
high-dimensional, in that there are almost always multiple possible actions or events that could be attributed to
reward.

org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The adult brain shows limited plasticity, which poses a challenge for learning new tasks in health, and for
repairing neuronal connections after injury such as stroke, or in neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer’s
disease. Most current approaches to increase plasticity cause permanent alteration of neuronal state, with
potential for detrimental outcomes, therefore new approaches are needed to transiently alter neuronal plasticity
state to enable controlled synaptic remodeling and repair. This is achieved by developing Targeted Degradation
of Extracellular Proteins (TDEP), a toolkit of genetic reagents to enable precise control of the neuronal
environment to promote brain health and repair that can be applied to multiple neurological disorders where
enhancing synap...

application_id: 11190128
activity_code: DP1
title: Epigenetic engrams in planarians
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Planarians regenerate their entire brain from scratch after decapitation. We observed that in the model
planarian Schmidtea mediterranea, individuals can be trained to associate chemical stimuli with a reward, and
that a memory of this training is maintained through decapitation and brain regeneration, consistent with
controversial studies from several decades ago. We will utilize modern molecular and genetic to identify the
chemical substrate of these trans-(re)-generational memories, with far-reaching implications for neuroscience
and epigenetics.

application_id: 11190860
activity_code: DP1
title: Micromanaging Human Sleep Physiology to Treat Sleep Apnea and Other Disorders
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Physical and mental health can suffer due to insufficient sleep, but even getting enough sleep can be
problematic when sleep is qualitatively suboptimal.
Suboptimal sleep contributes in the general case to problems that include drowsiness, lethargy, muscle aches,
inattention, and poor memory, and in various disease states to problems that include cardiovascular issues,
anxiety, depression, and other stress-related dysfunctions.
To improve health for a wide variety of patients, including but not limited to patients with obstructive sleep
apnea, this project will usher in a new orientation to improving sleep with innovative methods for altering sleep
physiology in the home based on easy-to-use wearable technology that changes what happens in the brain
during sleep.

application_id: 11360770
activity_code: DP1
title: Memory in the enteric nervous system
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
Memories in the central nervous system are an essential component of human physiology and behavior, but whether
memory exists in the peripheral nervous system, and in particular the enteric nervous system, remains unknown. This
proposal explores the concept of memory engram formation in enteric neurons. The results of this study have the
potential to transform our understanding of how the enteric nervous system contributes to numerous functions and
diseases of the gastrointestinal tract, ranging from infection and inflammation to metabolism.

phr: Project Narrative This renewal application for the Integrative Liver Cell Core (ILCC) proposes its continuation of unique and specialized services involving isolation and analysis of different liver cell types. As a national resource supported by the National Institute of Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism, the ILCC provides these services at the regional and national levels to support medical research on alcohol-associated liver diseases and liver fibrosis, which are prevalent in our nation and around the world. The long-term goal of the ILCC is to help scientists develop new treatments for alcoholic-associated liver diseases and cirrhosis.

application_id: 11190135
activity_code: DP1
title: De novo design and engineering of biological error correction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: NARRATIVE
Biology has evolved powerful error correction mechanisms to ensure the extreme fidelity of processes critical for
life and human health: DNA replication, protein translation, cellular proliferation, and immune signaling. Inspired
by nature, the planned research will develop generalizable strategies to build such error correction into human-
engineered systems to drastically increase specificity (correct versus incorrect outcomes) in therapeutic and
biotechnological applications. Examples range from highly sensitive diagnostics to tissue engineering to
advanced cell therapies.

activity_code: DP5
title: Bridging the gap between brain network science and high-definition non-invasive brain stimulation to develop a scalable adult literacy intervention
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this project is to address the urgent need for effective, scalable adult literacy interventions by
integrating breakthroughs in two separate fields: 1) the brain network science of resilience to reading disorders
and 2) high-definition non-invasive brain network stimulation. This study will first establish the efficacy of a
novel, non-invasive stimulation protocol on reading behavior and brain metrics; then will determine how
stimulation-induced effects interact with baseline reading comprehension ability; and lastly, will identify whether
stimulation-induced effects are more clinically-beneficial than canonical behavioral interventions. Results may
foundationally change how we treat low adult literacy, and have the potential for wider reaching impacts on
non-...

application_id: 11187412
activity_code: DP5
title: Female Sexual Dysfunction and Distress in the Preconception and Perinatal Period
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Knowledge gaps about female sexual dysfunction, distressing impairments in sexual function which affect an
estimated 40% of premenopausal women, are exacerbated by a lack of prospective longitudinal studies and
the fact that women are unlikely to discuss sexual dysfunction with healthcare providers. Using detailed data
about sexual function from a longitudinal study of pregnancy planners, supplemented by insights from in-depth
interviews, this project will develop a clinical tool to facilitate patient-provider discussions about sexual function.

application_id: 11380507
activity_code: DP5
title: Public Drinking Water Contaminants and Infant Health: Advancing Environmental Justice
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
The proximal mechanisms driving racial/ethnic and socioeconomic inequalities in infant health and their impact
across the lifespan remain poorly understood. The proposed project evaluates the contribution of inequalities
and injustices in public drinking water contaminant exposure to potential inequalities in in utero contaminant
exposures and subsequent inequalities in adverse birth outcomes. Characterizing the contribution of public
drinking water inequalities to racial/ethnic and socioeconomic health disparities can have major implications for
public drinking water regulatory policy in the US.

application_id: 11173827
activity_code: S06
title: ITCA NARCH 12 Admin Core
org_name: INTER TRIBAL COUNCIL OF ARIZONA, INC.
phr: NARCH XII Administrative Core Narrative 
The Inter Tribal Council of Arizona, Inc. (ITCA), is a tribally controlled non-profit organization 
established in 1975. ITCA has the goal of promoting American Indian self-reliance through public 
policy development, and to provide Member Tribes with the means for action on matters that affect 
them collectively and individually. The Member Tribes of ITCA are 21 federally-recognized Tribes 
located in the State of Arizona. The highest elected official of each Member Tribe serves on the 
council. These leaders have a comprehensive view of the conditions and needs of the tribal 
communities they represent. ITCA provides an independent capacity to obtain, analyze, and 
disseminate information vital to tribal community self-development.

activity_code: K22
title: Identifying Novel Intensity-Specific Regulators of RAS Signaling
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative RAS genes are mutated to remain in an active oncogenic state in one-fifth of all human cancers. The proposed study to identify novel intensity-specific regulators of oncogenic Ras signaling, and elucidate their roles during tumorigenesis has a direct relevance in a broad spectrum of human cancers. Hence, these regulators may predict the risk of KRAS-mutant cancers and could be leveraged as new therapeutic targets.

activity_code: K22
title: Defining the role of meningeal neutrophils in cognition
org_name: WEST VIRGINIA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project aims elucidate the role neutrophilsnplay in the homeostatic regulation of the CNS. Specifically, determining how neutrophils modulate neuronal function and ultimately an organism behavioral output, will greatly inform our understanding of their role in injury and neurodegeneration. The results of this project will not only define a new role for neutrophils, it will also define the meninges as a non-medullary source of these cells.

application_id: 10758201
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the Effects of Post-Translational Modifications on Tau Binding to F-actin and PSD95
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative  Alzheimer’s disease (AD) currently affects millions nationally with no clearly effective treatment that can target disease pathology. This proposal utilizes biophysical, structural and cell biological techniques to test a novel hypothesis that will aide our fundamental understanding of unique structure-function relationships of the protein tau by studying its canonical and disease-associated interactions. Our research will contribute to the field’s understanding of the mechanistic changes in tau behavior that drive AD and other tauopathies and will facilitate the development of novel therapeutic targets for the treatment of tau-based neurodegeneration.

application_id: 10861754
activity_code: K23
title: Utility of Centrally Acting Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors to slow the progression of Dementia
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative: The proposed study will examine if there are differential effects of angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor (ACEI) subtypes on 1) motoric, 2) cognitive, and 3) neurodegenerative and vascular outcomes among participants in 2 nationally available cohorts. It will be the first to examine these outcomes in older adults on ACEI without dementia. Early identification of older adults at risk for the development of cognitive impairment and functional decline, coupled with a better understanding of how this commonly prescribed medication affects cortical regions associated with dementia, is of paramount importance as a prelude to identification of targeted therapies to slow the progression of dementia.

activity_code: T32
title: Initiative to Maximize Student Diversity at the University of New Mexico Health Sciences Center 2021
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEW MEXICO HEALTH SCIS CTR
phr: This is a new application for a training program supporting predoctoral trainees in biomedical research at the University of New Mexico Health Sciences Center. Program strengths include a recruitment strategy targeted at institutions that serve students primarily from New Mexico and the Southwestern US, the formation of Learning Communities to support needs of trainees and career training in non-academic research areas. These strengths will enhance retention and increase potential careers paths open to IMSD trainees, preparing highly skilled biomedical scientists from New Mexico and the Southwestern US in biomedical research to contribute to the biomedical workforce.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Tuberculosis (TB) is the leading cause of mortality in people with HIV in sub-Saharan Africa, due partly to substantial delays in diagnosis caused by a lack of adequate clinic-based TB testing. The proposed research will evaluate a set of novel, point-of-care, rapid urine-based tests for TB in HIV-infected persons in an ambulatory clinical setting in South Africa, use results to develop improved diagnostic testing algorithms, and estimate the potential impact of introducing new diagnostic tests in different representative clinical use scenarios. title: Fever Etiology and Prognostic Point-of-Care Biomarkers in African Children with Severe Febrile Illness
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposed project will contribute vital knowledge regarding the risk factors, etiology, clinical course, and hospital outcomes in a population of children in sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) with severe febrile illness, without which providers cannot determine appropriate management, therapy, or resource allocation. application_id: 10747903
activity_code: K23
title: Systems approach to elucidate the microbiome's influence on the development of food allergies early in life
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Food allergy prevalence in children is dramatically rising and can be life-threatening while the biological explanations remain unclear. Utilizing a large prospective longitudinal healthy infant cohort I built to identify pediatric mucosal and allergic diseases, I propose to study the role of the intestinal microbiome in the development of one of the earliest manifestations of food allergy in children. Integrated analyses of comprehensive prospective clinical metadata, dense longitudinal biological sampling, and carefully defined allergic outcomes will allow us to identify important microbial and inflammatory pathways and immunologic responses, useful biomarkers, and novel targets for food allergy prevention.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The global burden of sepsis is concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa where epidemic HIV, broad pathogen diversity, and limited health system capacity challenge effective management of life-threatening infections. title: Immune, Microbial, and Metabolic Factors that Impact Clostridioides difficile and Inflammatory Bowel Disease in Children
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE C. difficile infections (CDI) and inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) are on the rise in children and commonly co- exist. This proposal seeks to better understand the determinants and sequelae of CDI and C. difficile colonization in children with IBD. Knowledge of these factors and their interactions can then be used in clinical settings to improve the treatment and outcomes associated with C. difficile in pediatric IBD patients.

application_id: 10896030
activity_code: K23
title: Optimal Dosing for Biologic Agents in Obese Patients with Rheumatoid and Juvenile Arthritis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In adults and children, drug dosing for biologic agents do not account for the substantial physiologic alterations resulting from obesity. These changes may result in a high inflammatory state and can affect drug disposition, thereby leading to therapeutic failure, increased disease activity, and poor clinical outcomes. This proposal will systematically characterize disease activity and biologic agent disposition in obesity using advanced pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic modeling, and use the model to optimize dosing and improve outcomes in inflammatory arthritis.

application_id: 10928095
activity_code: K23
title: Live video mind-body treatment to prevent persistent symptoms following mTBI
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative The proposed study will establish the feasibility, acceptability and credibility of a novel, live video mind-body program, “Toolkit for Optimal Recovery after Concussions; TOR-C”, aimed at preventing persistent symptoms following uncomplicated mild traumatic brain injury (aka mTBI) among college-age individuals with anxiety. This age group experiences among the highest prevalence of concussions, and up to 40% of these individuals have comorbid anxiety. A brief, tailored mind body program delivered to this high risk population during the window of opportunity of the acute phase has the potential to decrease conversion to costly and challenging-to-treat chronic symptoms of mTBI.

activity_code: K23
title: Mindfulness-Based Diabetes Education for adults with elevated diabetes distress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative Diabetes distress is common affecting over one-third of people with type 2 diabetes, negatively impacting self- management and outcomes, and disproportionately affecting low-income individuals. The proposed project will pilot test a Mindfulness-Based Diabetes Education intervention, specifically adapted for adults with type 2 diabetes and elevated diabetes distress, to assess feasibility and acceptability. Utilizing mixed-methods, this study will also characterize key contextual factors that relevant to intervention delivery and implementation setting the stage for a future, larger study in safety-net healthcare settings.

activity_code: K23
title: Immune mechanisms of Vitamin D in a randomized controlled trial to reduce chronic pain after burn.
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Chronic pain is a common, morbid sequela following burn injury. Currently, there are no therapies to prevent chronic pain development after burn injury; therefore, the goal of this research is to provide evidence on the effectiveness and corresponding immune mechanisms of vitamin D to prevent chronic pain following major thermal burn injury. This work has the potential to improve pain outcomes, understand important mechanisms through which vitamin D reduces pain, and provide new mechanistic insights into the transition from acute to chronic pain following burn injury.

activity_code: F30
title: Silencer Control of T cell Homeostasis
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative While silencers (cis-regulatory elements) are known to be highly cell type specific, their contribution to CD4 T cell homeostasis is unknown. This proposal seeks to identify functional silencer elements in CD4 cells, and provide a mechanism for silencer activation through PRC2 binding and H3K27me3 formation. This proposal aims to provide a catalogue of T cell specific silencer elements, the defining histone marks, and transcription factor regulation of silencers in T cells.

application_id: 10734067
activity_code: F30
title: Statistical developments for biomarker and diagnostic test evaluation with applications to Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Statistical methods used in agreement studies contain fundamental flaws that hinder the complete development and understanding of biomarkers and diagnostic tests. As we have entered into an era where neuroimaging biomarkers are warranted for use in the diagnostic strategy of Alzheimer’s disease, these statistical methods have never been more important. This project develops a novel statistical methodology for evaluating inter- and intra-rater agreement upon which diagnostic tests and biomarkers are evaluated, and, in doing so, will identify clinical, provider and neuroimaging features important for obtaining an Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis.

application_id: 10995316
activity_code: K23
title: Affective Executive Functioning as a Mechanism of Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy: A Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial
org_name: BUTLER HOSPITAL (PROVIDENCE, RI)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) is an efficacious treatment for patients with depression and those at risk for depression relapse, though the processes through which MBCT achieves its outcomes are not well understood. By bringing together two parallel research literatures on basic cognition and cognitive biases in depression, this project aims to examine whether affective executive functioning serves as a mechanism of action of MBCT via a pilot randomized controlled trial. Better understanding how MBCT works will help us to better identify individuals who may benefit from MBCT and further personalize MBCT to target such processes directly.

application_id: 11004616
activity_code: K23
title: Defining stress REsilience And Mindfulness Effects in Rheumatoid Arthritis (DREAMER)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A critical need exists for non-pharmacologic interventions that improve outcomes in patients with chronic autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA). This study will leverage infrastructure and resources from an RO1-funded cohort to longitudinally examine the effects of psychological stress, stress resilience, and mindfulness on RA disease activity and symptom severity. These findings will provide mechanistic targets for mindfulness-based interventions in RA, and inform a pilot randomized controlled trial of mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) for improving outcomes among people with chronic autoimmune conditions.

application_id: 10788353
activity_code: K23
title: Development and feasibility of a mind-body intervention to improve physical activity for patients with chronic hip pain
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Improving psychosocial health for patients with chronic hip pain has a strong potential to increase engagement in physical activity and thereby reduce pain and improve physical function and well-being. The proposed Helping Improve PSychosocial Health (HIPS) mind-body intervention delivered by physical therapists through a hybrid in-person-telephone modality may be an effective and scalable solution to improve physical activity in rural populations through psychologically informed rehabilitation. Consistent with NCCIH priorities, this project may promote a paradigm shift by providing an effective treatment care model through psychologically informed rehabilitation to promote whole joint health and patient well-being for the millions of patients with chronic hip pain.

activity_code: K23
title: Pain Disengagement Training: A self-directed intervention for pain catastrophizing
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed study will evaluate the feasibility and acceptability of “Pain Disengagement Training,” a novel, self-directed intervention for chronic pain. PDT targets pain catastrophizing, including the tendency to magnify the intensity of and ruminate on the experience of pain, which is a key predictor of the extent to which pain interferes in an individual’s life. Due to its self-directed nature, PDT has great potential to reduce the burdensomeness of pain in a large number of patients suffering from this chronic and often disabling condition.

org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE

title: Primary Progressive Aphasia: Beyond the Clinical Variants

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Deterioration of speech and language abilities is the main manifestation of primary progressive aphasia (PPA) and a frequent symptom in related neurodegenerative disorders, such as frontotemporal and Alzheimer's dementias. This proposal will use neuroimaging analyses and multilingual cognitive assessments to detect and diagnose speech and language impairment in diverse patient populations. These methods will provide new knowledge regarding the neural basis of speech and language and result in better detection, refined diagnosis and improved treatments for individuals with speech and language symptoms due to PPA and related neurodegenerative disorders.

application_id: 10808335
activity_code: K23
title: The Impact of the Auditory Environment on Auditory Processing of Maternal Voice and Auditory Cortex Maturation in Preterm and Term Infants
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Preterm infants are at risk for impaired language development due to physiologic insults, white matter injury, and the atypical auditory environment of the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit. The purpose of the proposed work is to investigate the influence of the auditory environment on attention to and auditory recognition memory for maternal voice as well as auditory cortex development in premature infants and fetuses subsequently born at term while accounting for variables known to impact language development. The ultimate goal of this work is to apply these findings to the design and implementation of interventions in the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit, which promote experience-dependent plasticity and improve the long-term outcomes of premature infants.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE End-stage liver disease places a disproportionately large burden on the healthcare system with certain individuals experiencing frequent repeat hospitalization and emergency room visits. The studies proposed will lead to accurate prediction of this vulnerable group by using multi-dimensional data and will establish the feasibility of using novel mobile phone program to improve healthcare use in liver disease. The proposed studies will bring the field closer to providing care that is (1) more aligned to patient goals, (2) adheres to current guidelines, (3) equitable and (4) at a lower cost.
