activity_code: K23
title: Metabolic and Neurological Changes Induced by a Very Low Carbohydrate Diet in Youth with Type 1 Diabetes
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  As more adolescents with type 1 diabetes (T1D) opt to follow very low carbohydrate diets to improve glucose control, it is becoming increasingly important to study the metabolic and neurological changes that occur on these diets and how these effect patient safety. Understanding the impact of dietary changes on metabolic and neurological factors will help guide clinical advice about the overall safety and efficacy of these diets in the pediatric T1D population. This work will be the basis of future studies testing dietary interventions to improve glycemic control and ensure that these dietary interventions are safe for growing youth with T1D.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Advanced management of children with type 1 diabetes (T1D) aims to utilize diabetes devices, such as insulin pumps (IP) and continuous glucose monitors (CGM), to optimize glycemic control; however, use of these devices has not resulted in the expected improvements in T1D outcomes. application_id: 10893331
activity_code: K23
title: Furosemide in severe bronchopulmonary dysplasia: comparative effectiveness of a duration and dosage informed treatment strategy
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), or chronic lung disease of prematurity, is the most common major morbidity of premature birth. Despite an absence of clinical research evidence to support its use, the diuretic furosemide is commonly used to treat infants with severe BPD, the worst disease severity class. This proposal seeks to address fundamental knowledge gaps in our understanding of furosemide treatment strategies in severe BPD, in order to subsequently design definitive clinical trials to support or reject its use in this vulnerable infant population.

application_id: 10864030
activity_code: K23
title: Aerobic exercise to improve mobility in multiple sclerosis: optimizing design and execution for a full-scale multimodal remyelination clinical trial
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a common neurologic disease caused by inflammation and damage to myelin, the fatty substance that insulates nerve fibers. Myelin repair may be an effective strategy to improve mobility and prevent disability in people with MS and, in animal models of MS, aerobic exercise has been shown to repair myelin independently or synergistically with medications. This project will investigate how damage to myelin impacts mobility in people with MS and will help design a future, full-scale trial using aerobic exercise with myelin repair medications to overcome and prevent MS disability.

application_id: 10880235
activity_code: T32
title: Training in Auditory and Vestibular Neuroscience
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Hearing and balance disorders affect a large percentage of our population. About 30-35 percent of adults between the ages of 65 and 75 years have a hearing loss, and it is estimated that about 50 million US citizens experience tinnitus in their lifetime. Balance disorders are also prevalent in the population, particularly amongst the elderly. More than one in three people age 65 years or older fall each year, and falls are the leading cause of hospitalization amongst the elderly. The proposed program will train the next generation of scientists to provide cures for hearing and balance disorders.

application_id: 10920377
activity_code: K23
title: Developing a reproductive health curriculum for adolescent girls with physical disabilities
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE With improved survival, more girls with born with physical disabilities are entering puberty, developing intimate relationships, and going on to have children. Although their disability impacts both their sexual health (e.g., allergy to latex condoms) and the way they learn (e.g., executive functioning limitations), they are not being educated about their sexual health, leading to poor long-term outcomes such as unintended pregnancies and low use of contraception. This project focuses on developing a user-centered, online sexual health educational intervention for girls with physical disabilities tailored to their unique learning style that addresses their highest priority sexual health topics.

title: The UW Medical Student Addiction Research (MedStAR) Program to Address Substance Use and Disorders in Urban and Rural Communities in Five Western States
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This application proposes to enhance the UW Medical Student Addiction Research (MedStAR) program, which
has been successful in creating opportunities for mentored research and education for medical students in 5
western rural-serving states (WA, WY, AK, MT, and ID) with a goal of generating a “pipeline” for medical

physician-scientists. In the next cycle we will enhance the program by bringing in the perspectives of persons
with living/lived substance use disorder experience to research, providing mentoring skills training and support,
and creating systems for tracking longer term outcomes of program success.

activity_code: R25
title: MD Anderson Summer Program in Cancer Research (SPCR)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Summer Program in Cancer Research will provide a 10-week, authentic, hypothesis-driven, hands-on,
cancer research experience to each of 17 talented undergraduate students, early in their professional paths, to
promote careers and/or further education in cancer and epigenetics research, health care, and oncology. Under
the direction of faculty and laboratory staff mentors, students will learn laboratory techniques and research
procedures as they gain exposure to innovative research tools and resources. This experience/training will
increasing their technical acuity, communication skills and establishing the self-confidence required to advance
in the field of cancer research.

application_id: 10879759
activity_code: K23
title: Developing a Self-Management Intervention to Improve Health Outcomes for Patients with Inflammatory Bowel Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To decrease symptom burden and maximize their quality of life, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients need to master skills that facilitate self-management—the daily behaviors individuals must perform to keep their illness under control, minimize its impact on their physical health, and cope with comorbid or resulting psychological symptoms. In alignment with the National Institute of Health’s mission to improve people’s health and quality of life, this study proposes to develop an intervention that is designed to support patients with active IBD in self- management and improve their care, symptoms, and quality of life. This approach will provide insights for feasible and acceptable self-management interventions with adaptability to diverse IBD populations.

application_id: 10847346
activity_code: K23
title: Molecular Markers of Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis Progression
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A major problem facing clinicians is their inability to identify idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) patients who will experience rapid progression before irreversible loss of lung function occurs. The proposed research will fill a significant void in pulmonary medicine through integration of longitudinal clinical data with chromatin accessibility and gene expression to uncover biologically relevant pathways that drive, or result from, disease course variability. The results will form an innovative platform in which to explore the biologic underpinnings and identify patients at high-risk for rapid IPF progression.

application_id: 11094482
activity_code: K23
title: Cardiovascular health assessment & outcomes of systemic lupus erythematosus: bridging pediatric and adult- onset disease
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Lupus is one of the top 10 leading causes of non-accidental death in adolescent females. Children and adolescents with lupus develop early blood vessel injury that significantly increases their risk of early heart attacks and strokes in adulthood. We currently do not know how to monitor or prevent these changes in childhood-onset lupus, which is more severe and confers greater cumulative tissue damage than adult-onset lupus. The proposed project seeks to determine whether blood pressure patterns and blood vessel function can be used to evaluate vascular health in children with lupus, and to assess whether medications used to prevent heart disease in other conditions may be beneficial in adolescents and young adults with lupus.

title: Characterization of B Lymphocyte Deficiency in Pediatric Sickle Cell Disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The clinical implications of B cell deficiency in SCD are not fully understood and requires investigating both the clinical implications and the mechanism of B cell deficiency. application_id: 11071332
activity_code: F30
title: Mapping the neural and physiologic response to tonic pain with low-intensity focused ultrasound: a bridge towards therapeutic applications
org_name: VIRGINIA POLYTECHNIC INST AND ST UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The experience of pain is a complex mixture of brain, autonomic, and behavioral responses, and the posterior insula (PI) and dorsal anterior cingulate (dACC) are key brain regions for pain and autonomic processing. Low- intensity focused ultrasound (LIFU) can safely, selectively, and non-invasively modulate deep brain regions, providing first-in-human capabilities for causally mapping the PI and dACC to better understand their function. This project will first leverage brain and multimodal autonomic measures during a tonic, pain-inducing stimulus to interrogate the neural and physiologic responses to pain and then use LIFU to map the causal role of the PI and dACC in these responses, laying the groundwork for future therapeutic interventions in chronic pain.

application_id: 10877727
activity_code: K23
title: The Von Willebrand Disease Aging and Bleeding Correlation (VWD ABC) Study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative  Patients with von Willebrand disease (VWD) are at increased risk for bleeding. Age may mitigate this risk. Little evidence is currently available to guide treatment in VWD patients whose von Willebrand factor levels have normalized with age. An enhanced understanding of the bleeding risk in older patients is critical because if they no longer bleed, VWD-specific therapy is costly and may increase the risk of thrombosis. Alternatively, if they continue to bleed, VWD-specific therapy may still be indicated. This study will help provide important new insights into this problem and provide more evidence to guide treatment and improve patient care.

title: PRIMING THE PUMP: TRAINING PHYSICIAN-SCIENTISTS IN TRANSLATIONAL PSYCHIATRY
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal aims to continue our highly successful research track program by recruiting and retaining
MD/PhDs and medical graduates with substantial research backgrounds into translational research
careers in psychiatry. This four-year program provides residents with extensive training and protected
time for mentored research, rendering residents better prepared for research fellowships and the K award
process, ultimately leading to careers as independent investigators. With ongoing innovations, we provide
additional support for those without a robust research background in efforts to expand the pipeline of
physician scientists pursuing translational research, a key goal of the NIMH.

activity_code: K23
title: Early Administration of Steroids in the Ambulance Setting: An Observational Design Trial
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Program Director/Principal Investigator (Last, First, Middle): Fishe, Jennifer, N Project Narrative This pilot cluster randomized controlled trial of EMS-administered CS for pediatric asthma patients will help fill a knowledge gap in the EMS treatment of this common pediatric emergency condition. This study innovatively combines clinical, economic, and geospatial analysis to see how EMS-administered CS affects patient outcomes, healthcare costs, and EMS operations. That novel combination of analyses lays the foundation for developing adaptable pediatric asthma guidelines for implementation by diverse EMS agencies nationwide as part of a future multicenter trial. application_id: 10739796
activity_code: K23
title: Leveraging Home Health Aides to Improve Outcomes in Heart Failure
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative One million patients are hospitalized each year for the management of heart failure, and 25% of Medicare beneficiaries who are hospitalized for heart failure are readmitted within 30 days. Previous interventions designed to prevent readmissions in this population have had mixed results, possibly because they have not involved home health aides, who are often with patients post-discharge for prolonged periods of time. The goal of the proposed project is to develop and pilot test a novel intervention for home health aides caring for patients with heart failure, in order to avert avoidable readmissions and improve patient outcomes.

activity_code: R25
title: Research Career Institute in Mental Health of Aging (CIMA)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VERMONT & ST AGRIC COLLEGE
phr: Project Narrative
The Research Career Institute in Mental Health of Aging (CIMA) is a national mentoring network that promotes the development of early career investigators whose research expertise includes brain-based mechanisms of mental illness, novel treatment and prevention approaches for aging individuals. In its first funding cycle CIMA has prepared postdoctoral scholars and new faculty for research careers focused on the mental health of aging. In this renewal we have transformed CIMA in response to advancements in science and mentoring, National Academies of Science, Engineering and Medicine recommendations and NIH priorities.

application_id: 10915039
activity_code: K23
title: Prognostic biomarkers for respiratory failure post hematopoietic cell transplantation
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Bone marrow transplantation can cure many cancers, blood disorders and metabolic or genetic diseases; however, it can be accompanied by life-threatening pulmonary complications. Respiratory failure can be extremely severe, carries a mortality rate up to 60%, and has little directed treatment outside of supportive care. We propose to study a novel method to predict which patients are at the highest risk to develop respiratory failure using objective biomarkers that may allow for early identification of risk, development of new drugs for respiratory failure based on the biologic pathways identified with biomarkers, and ultimately targeted therapies to prevent the occurrence of respiratory failure.

application_id: 10894813
activity_code: K23
title: Shared Decision-Making to Improve the Health Status of Patients with Claudication: Developing and Implementing Strategies to Individualize Treatment Decisions
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Invasive procedures to open arteries supplying the legs for patients with pain and peripheral artery disease (PAD) include risks of complications and uncertainty around individual benefits. Aligning goals and values between patients with PAD and available treatment options requires better evidence of patient-centered outcomes and a deeper understanding of the decision-making process. The proposed work leverages complementary methods in advanced statistics and decision science to address these gaps, enabling clinicians and patients to make more informed, personalized, and goal-concordant decisions about PAD care.

activity_code: K23
title: Bag-Mask Ventilation to PreVent Hypoxemia during Tracheal Intubation in the Emergency Department: A Clinical Trial
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Thousands of critically ill adults require placement of an endotracheal tube in emergency departments each year, with up to one-in-five experiencing hypoxemia and one-in-forty experiencing cardiac arrest. Yet the optimal approach to providing bag-mask ventilation, the most effective tool in preventing hypoxemia, remains unknown in this setting. Therefore, we designed the SAFEty of bag-mask VENTilation in the ED (SAFE- VENTED) study, to determine the effect of bag-mask ventilation on hypoxemia during tracheal intubation in the ED and to provide critical preliminary data for a future, definitive, multicenter trial evaluating the effect of bag- mask ventilation on hypoxemia, aspiration, and clinical outcomes.

application_id: 11078813
activity_code: K43
title: A comprehensive whole genome sequence scan for positive selection in southern Africa
org_name: STELLENBOSCH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The influence of positive natural selection in shaping the genomes of southern African populations is largely
unknown. In this study, using the most appropriate and accurate statistical method, we will scan large-scale
whole genome sequencing datasets (> 1000 individuals from ~5 populations) for signals of positive natural
selection and characterize the functional importance of identified signals. Ultimately, we aim to detect novel
positive selection signals that could have a potential impact on various human phenotypes in a largely
understudied population.

application_id: 10931336
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering Mechanisms of Racial Inequalities in ADRD: Psychosocial Risk and Resilience Factors for White Matter Integrity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Evaluating the impacts of interpersonal- and societal-level stressors on brain health is important for understanding the mechanisms that may be driving stark racial disparities in Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD). The proposed project will examine the impact of racially patterned social stressors on white matter integrity, an indicator of brain health and ADRD risk that may be particularly relevant for minority populations. It will also quantify the extent to which psychosocial stressors explain racial disparities in white matter integrity and whether neighborhood social cohesion can mitigate the impact of these stressors. Results of the proposed study could lead to more targeted interventions to reduce ADRD risk and racial disparities.

title: The Impact of Morning Insulin Delivery on Hepatic Glucose Metabolism Later in the Day
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diabetes affects nearly half a billion people worldwide and is correlated with an increased risk for cardiovascular disease, the leading cause of death globally. The proposed studies will provide novel insight into hepatic glucose handling following sequential meals to provide a more thorough understanding of the second meal phenomenon that can then be utilized to devise strategies aimed at improving glycemic management over a 24h period. This is incredibly important for individuals with insulin-dependent diabetes, as meal composition and timing can be tailored as a means of lifestyle intervention to maximize hepatic glycogen reserves, which will act as a first line of defense against hypoglycemic episodes.

title: Building a Sedation Minimization Culture
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Deep sedation of patients receiving mechanical ventilation in the intensive care unit (ICU) continues to be a
common practice nationwide despite decades of research demonstrating its harm. This proposal seeks to
develop a program of multifaceted implementation strategies (PROMISE-ICU) to increase the adoption and
fidelity of Spontaneous Awakening Trials, focusing on interprofessional team dynamics, communications, and
collaboration. We will conduct survey and qualitative research to characterize determinants of individual and
group behaviors around Spontaneous Awakening Trials and use rigorous implementation science approaches
to develop and pilot test PROMISE-ICU to reduce unnecessary deep sedation in the ICU.

activity_code: K23
title: Identification of Anterior Segment Structural Biomarkers in Glaucoma Following Pediatric Cataract Using Ultrasound Biomicroscopy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
This project seeks to identify the structural risk factors for glaucoma following congenital cataract surgery, a
devastating adverse event impacting 30% of children after cataract removal. Comprehensive normative
anterior segment structural data for infants and children does not currently exist. Ultrasound biomicroscopy
image analysis will be used to build a normative baseline anterior segment database for infants and children
with and without cataracts, which will be applied to identify structural risk factors or biomarkers for future
development of glaucoma, allowing for earlier diagnosis and targeted treatment.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR

application_id: 10877156
activity_code: K23
title: Cardiovascular outcomes related to nutrition policies among socioeconomically disadvantaged populations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Cardiovascular disease (CVD) remains the top cause of death in the U.S., and diet is the leading contributing factor to developing CVD. Racial, ethnic, and socioeconomically disadvantaged groups, who disproportionately rely on federal safety-net programs like the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) and Medicaid, are at higher risk for both poor diet and CVD. This proposed research will provide needed evidence about the diet and health of individuals receiving SNAP, and how SNAP intersects with CVD outcomes – which will help inform clinicians and policymakers on ways to better ensure high-quality, equitable nutrition that can prevent negative cardiovascular and other health outcomes among racial, ethnic, and socioeconomically disadvantaged Americans.

application_id: 11033091
activity_code: K23
title: Harnessing the Power of Social Connection to Improve the Quality of Life of Older Adults with Multimorbidity
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Multimorbidity is becoming increasingly prevalent in older Hispanics and is associated with functional limitations and psychological distress. To promote quality of life and high quality of care for all older adults, there is an urgent need to identify effective ways to enhance multimorbidity management among older adults with low socio-economic status (SES). This project will lay the groundwork for an independent research program that will harness the power of social connection to promote multimorbidity management for older adults in low SES aging communities.

application_id: 10855635
activity_code: K23
title: The Digital Phenotype of Bipolar Disorder: Harnessing Technology to Identify Bipolar Mood Symptoms - Revision - 1
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Bipolar disorder typically begins in adolescence; recurrent mood symptoms during this period can have a devastating impact on a person's ability to achieve a high quality of life. Digital phenotyping, defined as the “moment-by-moment quantification of the human phenotype in situ” using data collected from smartphone sensors, may make it possible to better understand and predict the onset of mood symptoms with minimal burden to the individual. The goal of this K23 proposal is to characterize the digital phenotype of bipolar disorder in adolescents in order to prospectively predict mood symptoms, creating an opportunity to reduce the harmful effects of recurrent episodes.

application_id: 10854809
activity_code: K23
title: Striatal Function in Psychosis Relapse
org_name: FEINSTEIN INSTITUTE FOR MEDICAL RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Symptom relapse is quite common in the course of illness of schizophrenia, yet its pathophysiology has not been well captured in most previous research, as few studies account for the common confounder of treatment non-adherence. In this proposal, we aim to study brain functioning in individuals treated with long acting injectable antipsychotics at the time of relapse, for whom medication delivery can be confirmed. The results from this study will generate knowledge about the essential brain mechanisms of psychosis relapse despite confirmed medication delivery.

application_id: 10805477
activity_code: K23
title: Digital Monitoring of Impulsivity as a Proximal Risk Factor for Suicidal Outcomes
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To advance prediction and prevention of suicide, a leading cause of death, we need more precise understanding of who needs what intervention and when through investigation of proximal risk factors, and impulsivity is an ideal candidate. The proposed research will use digital monitoring of patients at high risk for suicide to conduct the first intensive longitudinal assessment of multiple components of impulsivity and suicidal urges in real time among individuals at high risk for suicide. Findings will improve understanding of impulsivity as a transdiagnostic short-term risk factor for suicide, laying the groundwork for development of targeted interventions for suicide prevention, and may be generalizable to other impulsive behaviors.

title: Photophobia and Pupillary Autonomic Dysfunction as Biomarkers of Post-Traumatic Headache
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Post-traumatic headache (PTH) is the most common consequence of traumatic brain injury, and is frequently chronic and refractory to typical pain treatments. Autonomic and sensory dysfunction have been recognized in PTH and represent key, objectively measurable candidate disease markers. This proposal will use an innovative, multimodal approach to sensory and autonomic testing to identify clinically meaningful biomarkers for use in PTH diagnosis, disease monitoring, and the development of non-addictive treatments with the goal of reducing the overall health burden of PTH.

title: Developing and piloting a multilevel intervention to address psychosocial and structural syndemics in people with HIV in South Africa
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In South Africa, the country with the highest HIV prevalence (19%), comorbid problems such as depression, post-traumatic stress, and food insecurity synergistically enhance one another (i.e., syndemic problems) and are associated with worse HIV outcomes such as poorer antiretroviral therapy (ART) adherence and worse viral load. This study proposes to: 1) explore the complex interrelationships between syndemic problems and explore attitudes to potential intervention components; 2) develop a multilevel intervention to address syndemic problems and improve adherence (CBT-SA); 3a) assess the feasibility and acceptability of CBT-SA in a pilot RCT; and 3b) identify barriers and facilitators of CBT-SA engagement. activity_code: K23
title: Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction in Adolescents with Type 1 Diabetes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Anxiety symptoms occur in adolescents with T1D at 2 to 3 times the rate of the general adolescent population and are associated with out-of-range glycemic control, including severe hypoglycemia and diabetic ketoacidosis, poor quality of life, and poor self-management. There are few evidence-based interventions adapted and implemented to assist with alleviating anxiety for this high-risk population. Development of an adapted MBSR mHealth application prototype will provide accessible, cost-conscious, scalable access to a safe intervention, thus reducing cost and increasing access for this high-risk population.

application_id: 10867432
activity_code: K23
title: Decoding / encoding somatosensation from the hand area of the human primary somatosensory (S1) cortex for a closed-loop motor / sensory brain-machine interface (BMI)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE For patients with upper extremity dysfunction caused by strokes, brain trauma, or spinal cord injury, brain- machine interface (BMI) technologies may one day restore function by utilizing cortical neural signals to control the movements of a robotic limb with multiple degrees of freedom. However, the current generation of BMI devices are guided by visual feedback alone, without somatosensory feedback. Our proposal is designed to increase our understanding of sensorimotor integration by examining how varying stimulation parameters affect perceived somatosensation (Aim 1), examine task performance using artificial somatosensation versus native touch (Aim 2), and compare real touch to artificial somatosensation for response time (Aim 3).

application_id: 10817662
activity_code: K23
title: Speaking of Spikes: Connectivity and Language in Benign Epilepsy with Centrotemporal Spikes
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Children with benign epilepsy with centrotemporal spikes (BECTS), the most common pediatric epilepsy syndrome, have a mild seizure disorder but yet have moderate difficulties with language that impact their quality of life more than the seizures. Separate from the seizures, these children have frequent abnormal activity in their brain known as interictal epileptiform discharges (IEDs), which we currently do not treat. This project uses a form of non-invasive brain stimulation (called transcranial magnetic stimulation) to determine the impact of IEDs on brain regions important for language in order to investigate: (1) if treatment of IEDs could improve language; and (2) if brain stimulation may be a novel treatment option.

title: Decision Making for Infants with Neurologic Conditions
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY

title: Investigating Autonomic Dysfunction as an Early Pathologic Feature of Huntington’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative The overarching goal of this project is to determine if dysfunction of the autonomic nervous system (ANS) is one of the earliest pathological features of Huntington's Disease (HD). title: Uncovering the neurophysiology of motivation in ParkinsonÃÂs disease with implanted adaptive brain stimulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Personalized, adaptive, brain stimulation has the potential to benefit disabling behavioral symptoms such as apathy and impulsivity across a wide range of neurological conditions, including Parkinson’s disease, but is currently limited by a lack of understanding of how the brain assesses rewards to drive actions. The goal of this project is to determine how rewards are evaluated across the frontal cortex - basal ganglia circuits and to evaluate the mechanistic role of the structures and signals of this network in Parkinson’s disease. This foundational understanding will direct future stimulation therapies to reduce the burden of apathy and impulsivity in Parkinson’s disease and other neurological disorders in a precise and principled manner.

title: Mentoring in Immunometabolic Dysregulation in TB and TB/HIV
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Tuberculosis (TB) remains among the most deadly infections worldwide, especially among people living with HIV. Current available tests are not able to accurately detect persons at the highest risk of developing TB, and curing the disease requires at least 6 months of therapy because the TB bacteria can avoid being killed by the immune system and currently available drugs. In the current proposal, physician scientists will receive training in a variety of complementary disciplines, and use several cutting-edge experimental and modeling techniques to analyze samples from patients with TB and TB/HIV, with the ultimate goal of identifying new biomarkers that can predict TB disease and host-directed therapies that can shorten TB treatment.

application_id: 11081733
activity_code: DP1
title: Long-Acting Injectables for Treatment of HIV among PWID (LIFT HIV)
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: 8. PROJECT NARRATIVE This application seeks to explore the efficacy, safety, acceptability and cost-effectiveness of long-acting antiretroviral therapy (LA ART) on viral suppression among virally unsuppressed people who inject drugs in New Delhi, India. If successful, this application will generate evidence to support a new indication for the use of LA ART and transform the delivery of ART in a disenfranchised vulnerable population with high mortality.

title: Expansion of the  efficacy trial of Tele-Health Concierge into a federally qualified health center operated low barrier clinic
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE

application_id: 11504349
activity_code: DP2
title: THWART-TB : Testing Health Workers At Risk to advance our understanding of TB infection
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
It has been estimated that 1.7 billion people have tuberculosis (TB) infection; yet current
tests are unable to predict which people are at highest risk of developing TB disease,
which can be life-threatening. This project will use a range of novel tests to characterize
the profile of immune responses to TB over time in a high-risk population of South
African health workers. The knowledge generated will improve our understanding of TB
infection and help to identify which people exposed to TB may remain at risk, enabling
us to better target preventive strategies.

application_id: 11128597
activity_code: R00
title: Behavioral and neurocognitive mechanisms linking peer victimization to adolescent psychopathology
org_name: NORTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Peer victimization is a common and potent predictor of psychopathology in adolescence, but the behavioral and
neural mechanisms underlying this association remain unknown. I will test a novel conceptual model assessing
how dimensions of peer victimization—peer threat and peer deprivation—differentially shape neurocognitive
processes and social behaviors that confer risk for internalizing psychopathology. Identifying developmental
processes that are disrupted following peer victimization and ultimately lead to psychopathology is a necessary
first step in developing targeted intervention approaches to mitigate the effects of peer victimization on youths.

This study is designed to help improve the care of SGM with AD/ADRD in LTSS by conducting a rigorous study
of the policies of LTSS agencies in Minnesota and by training the state’s LTSS workforce in SGM culturally
responsive care. The study of policy will inform whether LTSS agencies need regulation or have already
adopted SGM appropriate policies. The randomized controlled trial will rigorously test the effects of an in-
person versus an online training program on improving SGM culturally responsive care.

application_id: 11169886
activity_code: R00
title: Linking genomic, physiological, and behavioral responses using a Drosophila model of heavy metal stress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA
phr: Contact PD/PI: Everman, Elizabeth
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mapping quantitative trait variation to specific genes has proved critical for our understanding of the genetic basis
of disease in humans, and the same approaches can be used to identify genes and networks that are responsible
for variable resistance to metal poisoning in natural populations. Here I will combine powerful, highly replicated
genetic mapping with multiple behavioral and physiological assays and whole genome and RNA sequencing to
characterize the genetic basis of resistance to copper poisoning. By exploiting an elite model genetic system,
this work will provide novel insight into genetic variants that influence susceptibility to metal poisoning in humans.

activity_code: R01
title: Landis Award for Outstanding Mentorship 2025
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS SAN ANTONIO
phr: Project Narrative:
Disease, degeneration or traumatic injury of the nervous system are among the greatest public health concerns
in the United States. Adult neural stem cells raise hope that functional regeneration of nervous tissue may be
feasible, if we understand the mechanisms that promote adult neurogenesis. This research will lead to improved
understanding of aberrant hippocampal neurogenesis, possibly leading to the development of new treatments
for mitigating epilepsy.

application_id: 11126735
activity_code: R00
title: Ameloblast-specific mineral ribbon attachment/elongation complex in enamel formation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mutations in basement membrane-associated genes cause amelogenesis imperfecta in humans. I will use
mouse models to study an ameloblast-specific mineral ribbon attachment/elongation complex that is composed
of these basement membrane-associated components. The completion of this proposal will advance our
understanding of enamel formation, shed light on the therapeutic interventions of amelogenesis imperfecta,
and provide insights for enamel biomimetics.

application_id: 11126611
activity_code: R00
title: Exploiting Metabolism to Uncloak Epstein-Barr Virus Immunogens in Latently Infected B-cells
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: Narrative:
As an orally transmitted pathogen, EBV translocates across the tonsillar epithelium and infects
B cells, whereupon reaching the memory B-cell compartment, it silences nearly all of viral
protein-coding genes to avoid immune detection. Here, we will investigate how lymphocyte
metabolism pathways are subverted to cloak EBV’s presence, and how interventions such as
restriction of the amino acid methionine can be used to reverse this silencing mechanism. The
research promises to provide new directions in treatment of EBV-driven disease.

application_id: 11412271
activity_code: R33
title: Multimedia caregiver education program to improve outcomes for children with cancer in low-resource settings
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Each year, 400,000 children are diagnosed with cancer, with 85% in low- and middle-income countries
(LMICs) where there is a 60% survival disparity compared to high income countries. The largest single
contributor to this disparity is treatment abandonment. The current proposal seeks to reduce treatment
abandonment through the adaptation and evaluation of two digital health education strategies targeting
caregiver knowledge about cancer diagnosis and treatment, reducing a known barrier to completing cancer
therapy and therefore improving outcomes for children with cancer in LMICs.

title: Cancer research education program (C-REP)
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative: TRAINING AND EDUCATION PROGRAM Diverse investigators play an integral role in identifying novel research questions, perspectives, and solutions to complex biomedical challenges. Yet, diversifying the biomedical research workforce remains a major challenge in the US, despite the fact that underrepresented minorities make up the most rapidly growing segment of the US population. Thus, in a unique partnership between the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai and Hampton University, we propose to provide hybrid training and education to increase the research capacity, engagement, and success of the HU scientific workforce, with particular emphasis on genetics and genomics and cancer disparities research.

application_id: 11062877
activity_code: DP2
title: SINGLE-CELL CHEMICAL TRANSCRIPTOMIC DISSECTION OF AN ESSENTIAL TRANSCRIPTION FACTOR NETWORK
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
All cells must regulate genes in order to divide, differentiate and die. This proposal employs cutting-edge
genomic techniques to better understand how single cells use MYC-network transcription factors to make
these decisions in stem cell pluripotency. The results will inform our basic understanding of how cells divide
and differentiate in mammalian development and ultimately help understand how these proteins can
deregulate cellular growth during cancer.

application_id: 11091538
activity_code: K00
title: Modulation of alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor in HIV-infected microglia and brain organoids
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PUERTO RICO MED SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Cathepsin B is a lysosomal protease that plays a significant role in the neurotoxicity caused by HIV-infected macrophages, which are the main reservoirs of HIV in the brain. Cannabinoid receptor type 2 (CB2R) activation by agonists decrease cathepsin B secretion, neurotoxicity, HIV-1 replication, and inflammation in macrophages. Therefore, understanding the mechanisms of CB2R activation in macrophages and cathepsin B neurotoxicity will permit the development of alternative strategies against HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders (HAND).

application_id: 10891400
activity_code: DP2
title: Acceptance of non-self: Decoding intestinal immune tolerance during early life
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative The intestinal immune system has a unique ability to discriminate harmless commensal microbes from pathogens, yet remarkably little is known about the cell types that initiate tolerance to commensal antigens at the time of colonization in early life. A developmental wave of antigen-presenting cells (APCs) seeds the intestine during the first week of life. This proposal seeks to determine how these APCs establish tolerance to commensal microbiota, the tissue signals that drive their development, and their role in susceptibility to intestinal immune mediated diseases in adulthood.

application_id: 11112435
activity_code: DP2
title: Harnessing Stem-Like CD8 T Cells for Immunotherapies to Eradicate HIV Reservoirs
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
37.9 million people worldwide currently living with HIV/AIDS rely on costly lifelong cART treatment to suppress
HIV replication and are at risk of non-AIDS comorbidities as they age. Thus, there is a pressing need to develop
new strategies that induce long-term remission of HIV without the need for cART. This study will test a novel
strategy that harnesses the transcriptional and epigenetic programs of stem-like CD8 T cells to enhance the
efficacy of immunotherapies targeting the HIV reservoir and will lay the foundation for future therapies that
achieve a cure for HIV.

application_id: 11124001
activity_code: UE5
title: Stanford Neurosurgery and Neurology Resident Research Education Program
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Our active, engaged commitment to increasing the success of Neurosurgery and Neurology (NSci) residents well prepared for independent research careers, directly benefits the trainees, patients, and scientific communities we serve. application_id: 11124665
activity_code: DP2
title: Regulatory genomics of T cells in mouse and human
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
T cells coordinate systemic immunity and are essential for defense against pathogens. Systematic molecular
analysis of T cells and of T cell interactions with other cell types across tissues is fundamentally important for
better understanding of mechanisms of the adaptive immune system and critical for developing better T cell
modulation therapies for infectious diseases and autoimmunity. The goal of our research program is to employ
innovative experimental genomics to build multi-dimensional data resources and develop new computational algo-
rithms that we will use to obtain a comprehensive molecular understanding of T cell activation and differentiation
in response to various stimuli across biological contexts in mouse and human.

title: Genomic and Microenvironmental Determinants, Temporal Dynamics, and Treatment Efficacy of Radiation-Based Combination Therapies
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Overall Section
 This proposal focuses on elucidating the genomic and microenvironmental determinants, and temporal
dynamics underlying efficacy of radiation-based combination therapies. We will use an innovative molecular
characterization trial testing radiation plus antibody-drug conjugate in bladder cancer and radiation plus
immunotherapy in head and neck cancer to characterize the mechanistic drivers underlying these next
generation RT-based therapies. Our studies will help build a foundation to optimize multimodal, radiation-
based definitive treatment strategies.

application_id: 11139649
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the mechanism of herpesvirus genome packaging
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Herpesviruses are important human pathogens that can have devastating effects in neonates and
immunocompromised individuals, yet there is no cure for any herpesvirus and treatment options are lacking.
Here, I dissect the mechanism of an essential feature of the herpesviral lifecycle - viral genome packaging - by
exploring the role of a unique accessory factor required for packaging and by developing an assay to monitor
packaging in vitro. Detailed mechanistic insight is essential to understand the mechanism of current
antiherpesviral drugs and to facilitate the identification of new targets for antivirals.

title: Interferon-gamma mediates neuroinflammation, demyelination, and neurodegeneration in a mouse model of multiple system atrophy (MSA)
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This proposal seeks to elucidate the role of Th1 cells in multiple system atrophy (MSA), a debilitating, fast- progressing neurodegenerative and demyelination movement disorder with no current treatment options. title: Pathways Linking Negative Self-Views of Aging to Physical Activity in Daily Life: An Intensive Within-Person Approach
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MISSISSIPPI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Although regular physical activity comes with many health benefits, including lower risk of disability and chronic disease, higher functional independence, and greater psychological well-being, many US adults— especially those in later life—do not obtain sufficient levels of activity based on federally recommended guidelines. This project uses data captured repeatedly and intensively in daily life to examine whether negative beliefs about aging result in less physical activity by hampering exercise motivations and self-regulation. title: Supportive Care Needs in Asian Americans with Metastatic Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer incidence is expected to increase to 23.6 million by 2030 worldwide, and cancer is the leading cause of death for Asian Americans whose numbers are rapidly rising to reach 40 million by 2050. application_id: 11166481
activity_code: DP1
title: Reprogramming intestinal immunity in preterm neonates to prevent and cure necrotizing enterocolitis
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) is a devastating consequence of prematurity that results from an aberrant pro-
inflammatory immune response and leads to intestinal necrosis. In the United States, NEC has a mortality rate
that can approach 50% and is responsible for 1 in 10 deaths in the neonatal intensive care unit. My innovative
research program is dedicated to preventing and curing this deadly disease through studies focused on
mechanisms of disease pathogenesis, identification of early disease biomarkers, and development of targeted
preventative and therapeutic strategies to improve outcomes for these vulnerable neonates.

application_id: 11169973
activity_code: DP5
title: Defining the Autonomic Cerebellum in Autism
org_name: NORTHEAST OHIO MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There are no targeted treatments for the dysregulated autonomic responses (‘dysautonomia’) of autism; yet,
these disruptive physiologic responses that consist of alterations in heart rate, blood pressure, and perspiration
affect over 70% of autistic individuals and contribute to significant disability. Building on a novel finding that the
cerebellum, a brain region affected in autism, contains specific sites that control autonomic output, the proposed
research will determine which cerebellar sites selectively influence autonomic physiologic responses and identify
which sites are altered in a model of autism. Results are likely to advance our knowledge of the autonomic
cerebellum and identify specific regions to therapeutically target for the dysautonomia prevalent in auti...

application_id: 11168953
activity_code: DP2
title: BREATH - Breastfeeding, Early-life Microbiome and Respiratory Health Study
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will combine large birth cohorts, state-of-the-art algorithms, and animal models to elucidate the
intricate mechanisms through which breastfeeding and microbial colonization modulates childhood respiratory
health outcomes years in advance. Our study will revolutionize the way breastfeeding and early-life microbial
colonization are studied and understood and will provide a foundation for the development of robust microbiome-
based asthma predictors and targets for primary prevention.

activity_code: K38
title: Control of adipose immune-stromal niche topography in obesity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Obesity affects nearly half of Americans and is a risk factor for stroke, heart attack, and Type II diabetes. This investigation aims to uncover the signals that promote homeostasis in the visceral adipose tissue, which is intimately related to the adverse health effects of obesity. The findings will enhance our knowledge of the mechanisms promoting obesity and diabetes-related inflammation and inform the development of therapeutics to treat this inflammation and reduce or prevent obesity and diabetes-related adverse health outcomes.

application_id: 11171289
activity_code: DP5
title: Illuminating the spatial and temporal diversity of the gastrointestinal microbial ecosystem with label-free nonlinear optical microscopy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is one of many conditions where the microbiome plays an essential, but
poorly-understood, role, stemming from the lack of available tools to study the live microbiome. This project will
develop novel methods to improve the spatial and temporal resolution of label-free nonlinear optical
microscopy, enabling simultaneous imaging of host and microbe cells in the intestinal microenvironment in
mice in vivo with metabolic, biochemical, and structural contrast. This work will shed light on the microscopic
biochemical basis for IBD and open the door for studying complicated microenvironments with high spatial and
temporal resolution.

title: Long-read DNA and RNA sequencing to identify disease-causing genetic variation and streamline testing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although the widespread adoption of clinical exome sequencing has helped identify the genetic basis of many
previously unsolved Mendelian disorders, approximately 50% of individuals remain unsolved after a complete
clinical workup. Long-read sequencing has the potential to identify and resolve many complex or difficult-to-
identify variants such as insertions, deletions, repeat expansions, and variants within duplicated genes, but it
has yet to be systematically evaluated in a clinical cohort. Here I propose a set of experiments which seek to
evaluate the incremental diagnostic rate of long-read sequencing in cohorts that have had extensive but
nondiagnostic clinical genetic testing and to compare long-read sequencing as a single test to a standard
outpatient clinical wor...

application_id: 11171704
activity_code: K00
title: The Effects of Obesity on Limiting Brain Health and Stroke Recovery
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE After a stroke, individuals with sensory impairments have high instances of balance problems, falls, and lower extremity dysfunction, implicating the role of sensorimotor reorganization (i.e., plasticity). Here, we take an initial step in characterizing post-stroke sensorimotor plasticity and its relationship to walking using paired associative stimulation. The proposed work will lay the foundation for a career in which I aim to better inform lower extremity rehabilitation efforts by providing functional biomarkers as targets for modulation, and structural neural correlates to improve prognoses.

application_id: 11173104
activity_code: DP2
title: Genetic Engineering of Myeloid Cells to Treat Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Therapies derived from the adaptive immune system, such as T cells, have achieved
remarkable success in treating liquid tumors, but solid tumors remain a significant challenge.
This project will use innovative engineering strategies to harness the innate immune system,
which naturally infiltrates tumors and can both directly kill tumor cells and activate T cells
against cancer. By engineering key aspects of innate immune cells, we aim to create living
medicines capable of effectively combating solid tumors.

activity_code: K38
title: The impact of diet on granulopoiesis, neutrophil function, and sickle cell disease pathology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Millions globally are at risk of nutritional stress and these external signals serve as a potent regulator of human physiology and pathophysiology. It is largely unknown why sickle cell disease (SCD) presents with variable severity between patients. As much of the pathology in SCD is driven by neutrophils, we aim to evaluate how nutritional stress regulates neutrophils development, function, and contribution to SCD severity, with applications to other neutrophil-driven diseases.

title: Biologically Plausible Computational Models of Perirhinal Cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human behaviors seamlessly integrate perceptual and mnemonic information. To understand how the brain supports these abilities, this project will develop and evaluate biologically plausible computational models of medial temporal lobe-dependent behaviors, incorporating traditional neuroscientific methods within a deep learning framework. application_id: 11030284
activity_code: K00
title: Reversing age-related epigenetic changes and characterizing microglia heterogeneity in obesity and aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative In this project, we will determine whether plasma transfer approaches can reverse age-related epigenomic changes in microglia and identify genomic sites that could be targeted for mechanistic functional studies. We will also use single-cell molecular phenotypes, cell-specific translatomes, and microglia functional assays to characterize the dynamics of microglia populations in obesity and provide insights into aging- and obesity- associated neuroinflammation. Our long-term goal is to contribute mechanistic insights and knowledge into microglia aging and neuroinflammation and to develop novel strategies to promote healthy brain aging.

application_id: 11169089
activity_code: DP5
title: Structure and Pharmacologic Modulation of the Mitotic Chromosome's Central Axis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Little is known about the axial core, or “scaffold,” of the mitotic chromosome, and until recently its very
existence was in doubt. This proposal aims to establish basic principles of scaffold organization and to
develop tools for modulating scaffold assembly and function. Such tools will provide the basis for phar-
macological inhibition of scaffold formation, enabling the targeting of cancer cells in a mechanistically
novel manner.

application_id: 11224980
activity_code: K18
title: Developing a Comparative Single-Cell Spatial Transciptomic Atlas of Brain Aging in Non-Human and Humans
org_name: TULANE UNIVERSITY OF LOUISIANA
phr: Project Narrative
As people age, they often experience cognitive decline and an increased risk for neurodegenerative diseases
like Alzheimer's, which significantly impact quality of life. This research will create detailed, single-cell maps of
brain regions critical for memory and cognition—the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus—by comparing tissues
from humans and closely related non-human primates across different life stages. By identifying specific
molecular and cellular changes associated with aging, this study seeks to uncover pathways that could offer
promising new strategies for treating, or even preventing, age-related cognitive decline and neurodegenerative
diseases, ultimately improving brain health and quality of life for the aging population.

application_id: 11128200
activity_code: R01
title: Landis Award for Outstanding Mentorship: Administrative Supplement to NS131225
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative  Developing disease-modifying therapies for neurodegenerative diseases is notoriously diﬃcult and is exacerbated by the lack of accurate statistical models to identify the optimal time for intervention. Here, we will use ﬁve longitudinal datasets of Huntington disease, a genetic neurodegenerative disease, to propose new statistical strategies to accurately estimate the disease course. These strategies can be used to help researchers identify when an experimental therapy could best modify the disease course and design a well-powered clinical trial to test that therapy.

application_id: 11239866
activity_code: DP2
title: Unleashing T-cell anti-tumor response through repair  of altered RNA splicing and antigen mimicry recognition
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The most aggressive solid malignancies evade immune surveillance and destruction and are refractory to the
best available immunotherapies. Here we aim to evoke anti-tumor immunocompetence through 2 programs: 1)
Identification and repair of altered RNA splicing in T cells to unleash anti-tumor immunity, and 2) Restoring
anti-tumor function through vaccination against bacterial virulence factors mimicked by tumor antigens. These
programs are designed to shed light on how known biological mechanisms, never explored in the context of
immuno-oncology, could serve as therapeutic targets.

The 360° Collaborative Hub for AI in TMD Research (360° CHAT-TMD) aims to establish a national,
interdisciplinary, and patient-centered research initiative to address the critical need for interdisciplinary,
patient-centered approaches to advance understanding and treatment of temporomandibular disorders (TMD),
which affect millions and are often linked to chronic overlapping pain conditions. By partnering with the
University of Michigan, Harvard University, Mass General Brigham, and patient communities, the Hub will
integrate basic and clinical sciences, offer advanced training, and translate discoveries into evidence-based
treatments. Leveraging AI/ML technologies, the consortium will develop a robust data science infrastructure
and conduct interdisciplinary resea...

application_id: 11295774
activity_code: DP2
title: The Female Songbird as a Novel Mechanistic Model for the Neural Basis of Social Evaluation
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project narrative
While neuroscience is making rapid progress on how the brain encodes one’s own behavior, little is known, in
any model system, about how neural circuits evaluate another individual’s actions for proper social responses.
Our approach is to combine the unique ability of the female songbird to evaluate male song with state-of-the-art
behavioral, neural, and computational techniques to develop a novel mechanistic model for social evaluation.
Such a cellular and circuit-level understanding will lend valuable insight into disorders of social interactions such
as aphasias, agnosias, autism spectrum disorder, and various neurodegenerative diseases.

application_id: 11380408
activity_code: K00
title: Tau-mediated synaptic dysfunction in age-related neurodegenerative diseases
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: As the human population ages, the ability to maintain cognitive function with a brain accumulating tau pathology relies on the preservation and maintenance of synaptic networks. Therefore, understanding the mechanism by which tau contributes to network and synapse dysfunction is critical for developing preventative therapeutics. This proposal aims to determine the cellular mechanisms(s) by which pathologic tau drives neuronal network dysfunction and propose a training plan catered to the applicant that will promote success as an independent researcher in the field of aging research.

application_id: 10814360
activity_code: K00
title: Investigating the Role of Cell Plasticity in Malignant Transformation
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acquired resistance to targeted therapy poses a major barrier to curing cancer, but the molecular mechanisms that allow cancer cells to adapt to therapy remain poorly understood. I propose to elucidate mechanisms of resistance to multiple therapies in use or in trial to treat non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) by identifying novel regulators of the cell cycle and lung cancer cell identity. This work will provide novel insight into the principles that govern cancer cell plasticity and may help identify ways to improve the efficacy of targeted therapies in NSCLC and other cancers.

application_id: 11087674
activity_code: DP1
title: Multiomics and Antiretroviral Drug Exposure for assessing Metabolic Effects (MADE-FOR-ME)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Over 38 million people in the world live with HIV and are at greater risk of developing cardiovascular diseases,
even when receiving treatment for HIV. Because people living with HIV receive medications that affect
the body’s metabolism towards developing cardiovascular diseases, we will use state-of-the-art
methodologies to determine the relationship between exposure from these drugs and important metabolism
markers. We will identify the effects of drug exposure and pioneer a new area of HIV/AIDS research and more
reliably predict the development of cardiovascular diseases for those living with HIV.

title: Epigenomic labeling of cells that drive drug abuse behavior
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Opioid use disorder causes persistent epigenomic changes in brain reward regions that contribute to drug
seeking behavior, but we currently do not have a way to access these epigenomically altered cells selectively.
The proposed work develops a novel viral approach that leverages the unique epigenomic environment in animal
models of opioid use disorder to gain selective access to and control of the cells that drive drug seeking behavior.

application_id: 11062733
activity_code: DP2
title: Unraveling the developmental logic of cortical long-range projections using in situ sequencing-based neuroanatomy
org_name: ALLEN INSTITUTE
phr: Project Narrative
In mammalian brains, neuronal types defined by gene expression do not strictly correspond to those defined by
long-range projection patterns. This lack of orderly relationship raises the question of how complex circuits are
wired from basic neuronal types and how basic neuronal types can be defined beyond gene expression. This
proposal aims to unravel the wiring rules of cortical neuronal types defined by gene expression by mapping the
developmental relationship between projections and gene expression using innovative large-scale in situ
sequencing-based neuroanatomical techniques.

title: Diabetes Promotes Breast Tumor via Glycation-Induced Mechanical Changes in ECM
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Patients with comorbidity of diabetes mellitus and breast cancer have been shown to be at a greater risk of poor prognosis and death than non-diabetic patients. However, the interplay between tumor progression and diabetes is still mechanistically unclear. title: Integrative approaches to dissection of endocrine communication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative
 Endocrine interactions have been demonstrated as causal and contributory to most common, most

application_id: 11160662
activity_code: DP5
title: Molecular tools to decipher communication across the blood-brain barrier
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: Project Narrative
The blood-brain barrier impedes the effective treatment of nearly all brain disorders, and its dysfunction is a
leading cause of death. I propose developing and applying new tools to tag, track, and identify which native
proteins and cells in the blood can traverse the blood-brain barrier and learn the rules by which they do so.
This will expand many-fold the set of therapeutic pathways into the brain, reveal new targets to block disease-
provoking neuroinflammation, and provide a new understanding of how communication across the blood-brain
barrier supports brain health.

org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRDs) are a leading cause of death and disability in the United States, and while neuronal circuits in brain areas involved in learning and memory consolidation are known to be affected, how and why this occurs remains unclear. Hypertension- induced changes in the cerebral vasculature are proposed to contribute to cognitive impairment, yet the molecular and cellular mechanisms linking hypertension to dementia, particularly associative learning deficits, are still unexplored. application_id: 11167694
activity_code: DP2
title: Spatial and Temporal Resolution of EosinophilSpecialization in Allergic Microenvironments
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: NARRATIVE
Eosinophils are a key type of white blood cell in allergic disease that were historically characterized as only
promoting inflammation, but compelling evidence is emerging that eosinophils have a variety of functions in
distinct tissue environments. This proposal will research the range of eosinophil functions during allergic
disease, which may improve the understanding of how eosinophil-depleting drugs affect human health. This
work may substantially expand the role of eosinophils to include resolving allergic inflammation and may inform
strategies to bring personalized and predictive medicine to the treatment of allergic, inflammatory, and
autoimmune diseases.

activity_code: K00
title: Profiling and leveraging bystander T cells within the tumor microenvironment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although T cells specific for tumor antigens have been a primary target of immunotherapies, tumor-nonspecific “bystander” T cells are often found in human solid tumors. Despite being a major component of the tumor infiltrate, little is known concerning the role of bystander T cells within solid tumors. This project seeks to define mechanisms that underlie bystander entry and function within solid tumors and leverage bystander T cells to enhance anti-tumor immune responses.

Activities related to the oil and gas industry release environmental contaminants, including reproductive

application_id: 11179270
activity_code: DP1
title: Functional convergence following disruption of diverse genes associated with cannabis use and major depression
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
In the United States, millions of people use cannabis and there is now convincing evidence linking its use with
depression and suicidal behavior. By combining human postmortem genomics, MPRA genetic engineering, and
human induced pluripotent stem cell derived brain organoids, we propose to test the molecular and functional
impact of cannabis use perturbing the genotype of MDD risk alleles. We hope to find insights into the mechanism
of disease initiation and progression of MDD in people with substance use disorders.

application_id: 11169823
activity_code: DP5
title: Dissecting the logic of mammalian gene regulation using synthetic biology and single-cell sequencing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The precise control of gene expression is crucial for the faithful orchestration of the developmental
program, but mis-expression of genes can be pathogenic in many contexts. The proposed work will
combine advances in large-scale DNA synthesis and single-cell sequencing to study the regulation of
genes that pattern the developing nervous system, but are also implicated in diseases such as
Alzheimer’s and cancer. Therefore, the results of this project will reveal fundamental principles of
development but will also inform disease mechanisms and potential therapies.

title: Identification of Sex Differences in Monocytes and Metabolism in the Maintenance of Chronic Muscle Pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Recent key studies have demonstrated that immune dysregulation is a defining component of fibromyalgia and chronic muscle pain. application_id: 11182731
activity_code: K00
title: Discerning mechanisms of semaphorin 7A-mediated tumor progression via immunoevasion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative We have shown that SEMA7A promotes multiple aspects of breast cancer progression, including proliferation, survival, invasion, and immune evasion. The F99 portion of this proposal (Aim 1) aims to investigate SEMA7A- mediated alterations to TAMs and T cells in the TME responsible for suppressing anti-tumor immunity and if targeting SEMA7A directly can impede tumor growth and immunoevasion. The K00 aspect of this application (Aim 2) proposes to identify novel mechanisms of immunoevasion in renal cell carcinoma and pancreatic ductal carcinoma, which may reveal novel targets for treatment to prevent metastases and prolong patient survival.

application_id: 11168740
activity_code: DP5
title: Transposable Element Interaction and Its Impact on Human Development and Health
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Functional assessment of how the ubiquitous transposable elements (TEs) affect human development and
health has posed a major challenge. This proposal aims to provide a novel framework to study how
interactions between the hitherto “junk” TE sequences can regulate pre-mRNA splicing to affect gene function,
and argues that such a mechanism can substantially affect both human development and evolution, and help
explain the genetic etiology of human diseases.

application_id: 11169017
activity_code: DP5
title: Identifying, Characterizing, and Targeting Regulators of B Cell Activation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
B cell signaling is central to the adaptive immune response, serving as the basis for generation of humoral
immunity to foreign agents. A multi-component co-receptor complex carefully regulates the strength of B cell
signaling, and dysregulation of signaling through this complex can lead to autoimmunity, immunodeficiency, and
B cell cancers. This research program will elucidate the molecular details of how the B cell co-receptor complex
functions, is regulated by novel accessory components, and will engineer new tools to modulate B cell function,
laying the foundation for new immunomodulatory therapeutics.

title: Deciphering the principles and mechanism of RNA structure
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
RNA viruses and diseases contribute to the death and poor health outcomes of millions of individuals globally
every year. Understanding the principles of RNA structure will help to characterize the key RNA structural
elements of these viruses and illnesses to inform therapeutic designs to disrupt their action. Furthermore,
optimizing RNA structure in mRNA vaccines and other RNA based therapeutics will lead to increased stability
and efficacy of these interventions as well.

application_id: 10910386
activity_code: DP2
title: Enhancing CAR T cell fitness using novel epigenetic reprogramming factors
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Engineered T cell therapies for cancer have demonstrated remarkable clinical responses in some patients, but
many fail to respond due to poor T cell function. Current approaches to enhance T cell function and/or mitigate
dysfunction are limited in their effectiveness and durability, and therefore new approaches are needed to help
engineered T cells stay in the fight against cancer. This project will develop a novel approach to fundamentally
reprogram T cells with enhanced tumor killing function, thereby paving the way for more efficacious cancer
therapeutics.

title: Cell-free DNA Analysis for Liquid Biopsy of Cancer
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research in the first phase of this proposal is to investigate a new methodology to capture and define a novel ultrashort circulating tumor DNA species in the plasma of lung cancer patients. application_id: 10910505
activity_code: DP2
title: Electrified cryo-EM: a new tool to capture metastable neuron structures during an action potential
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative:
While electrical current is the basis of biological signal transduction and fundamental to all life on this planet, the
structural conformation of biological components under electrical stimulus remains elusive. This proposal will
establish a novel technique, electrified cryo-EM, that will enable the first nanoscale studies of dynamic neuron activity
during an action potential stimulated by an external electrical impulse. Insights from this program will have significant
impact on informing next-generation neurostimulation treatments for neurological diseases.

application_id: 11089353
activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding the language of inflammation between central nervous system resident immune cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: NARRATIVE
Central nervous system (CNS)-resident astrocytes and microglia interact to regulate pro-inflammatory
programs that drive neurodegenerative diseases, but the pathways and molecules involved are largely
unknown. Understanding the language of inflammation is a central question in the field of Neuroimmunology
and may guide new therapeutic approaches for multiple sclerosis and other neurologic diseases. This proposal
applies two novel high-throughput methods to study microglia-astrocyte interactions during CNS inflammation.

application_id: 11130971
activity_code: DP2
title: A modular cell therapy platform for controlling immunological tolerance
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
In the United States, more than 24 million people suffer from autoimmune disease and over 600,000 people die
of cancer each year. These maladies are predominantly a consequence of inappropriate orchestration of
immunological tolerance, yet few therapies exist to precisely control the associated processes. In this project,
we develop an entirely new class of living cell therapies that home to sites of tolerance induction and reprogram
immune responses to fight cancer and quell autoimmunity.

title: Bridging cellular and systems neuroscience: synaptic dynamics underlying behavior
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE  Synaptic connections between neurons form the basis of network connectivity in the brain, and synaptic plasticity is widely believed to underlie adaptive behavior and learning. Here, we combine conceptual and methodological innovations to drive a pioneering exploration of the relationship between synaptic transmission and ongoing behavior.

application_id: 10923575
activity_code: DP1
title: Micromanaging Human Sleep Physiology to Treat Sleep Apnea and Other Disorders
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Physical and mental health can suffer due to insufficient sleep, but even getting enough sleep can be problematic when sleep is qualitatively suboptimal. Suboptimal sleep contributes in the general case to problems that include drowsiness, lethargy, muscle aches, inattention, and poor memory, and in various disease states to problems that include cardiovascular issues, anxiety, depression, and other stress-related dysfunctions. To improve health for a wide variety of patients, including but not limited to patients with obstructive sleep apnea, this project will usher in a new orientation to improving sleep with innovative methods for altering sleep physiology in the home based on easy-to-use wearable technology that changes what happens in the brain during sleep.

title: Identifying, Characterizing, and Targeting Regulators of B Cell Activation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE B cell signaling is central to the adaptive immune response, serving as the basis for generation of humoral immunity to foreign agents. A multi-component co-receptor complex carefully regulates the strength of B cell signaling, and dysregulation of signaling through this complex can lead to autoimmunity, immunodeficiency, and B cell cancers. This research program will elucidate the molecular details of how the B cell co-receptor complex functions, is regulated by novel accessory components, and will engineer new tools to modulate B cell function, laying the foundation for new immunomodulatory therapeutics.

title: Tapeworm stem cells as drivers of regeneration and reproduction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA

application_id: 10912053
activity_code: DP2
title: Shifting immunodominance of humoral immunity against influenza viruses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Current influenza viruses do not provide broadly protective immunity due to the immunodominance of humoral immunity against evolving epitopes of the hemagglutinin head domain rather than against the conserved stalk domain. In this study, we will rationally design and test a vaccine that shifts immunodominance of humoral immunity towards the stalk domain by increasing the diversity of head epitopes, which will allow stalk-specific B cells to more readily acquire antigen and be selected into the long-lived memory B cell pool. Using multiple models of preexisting immunity, our study will reveal that increasing head epitope diversity augments broadly protective humoral immunity.

application_id: 11014666
activity_code: DP2
title: Multifunctional phase sensors for probing and manipulation of intracellular biomolecular condensates
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Intracellular assemblies of intrinsically-disordered proteins (IDPs) are pathological hallmarks of untreatable
human neurodegenerative disorders. Preventing therapeutic innovation, current technologies fail to probe and
manipulate the dynamics of IDP-assemblies (e.g., their formation and maturation) and related biomolecular
condensates in their native tissue contexts. This proposal innovates intracellular protein-sensors of IDP self-
assembly, integrated with state-of-the-art brain organoids, to generate biophysical, biochemical, and
therapeutic insights into IDP-driven mechanisms of human neurodegeneration.
