title: Integrative approaches to dissection of endocrine communication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative  Endocrine interactions have been demonstrated as causal and contributory to most common, most notably obesity, type 2 diabetes and resulting cardiovascular complications. The goal of this proposal is to closely bridge computational and experimental methods to systematically dissect mechanisms by which tissues communicate and how these interactions are perturbed in disease settings. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA

application_id: 10915883
activity_code: DP1
title: Gut microbiota-mediated fiber fermentation in HIV-associated cardiovascular disease
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:  Hypertension and cardiovascular disease occur at substantially greater rates in optimally treated people with HIV, though the etiology of this elevated comorbidity risk remains poorly understood. The gut microbiota of people with HIV exhibits a loss of microbes that metabolize dietary fiber into key metabolites that can ameliorate hypertension. We will investigate the dynamics of fiber metabolism and uptake in the setting of HIV, the role of these phenomena in cardiovascular comorbidities, and whether capacity for fiber metabolism can be restored within the HIV-associated gut microbiome.

application_id: 10839337
activity_code: K00
title: Elucidating and exploiting NAD metabolic defects in cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Lung cancer is the most commonly diagnosed and deadliest cancer in the world, accounting for over nearly one-fifth of all cancer deaths in 2018 and the 5-year survival rate for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is only 18.1%. The current proposal will focus on the link between cancer redox metabolism and DNA damage repair/signaling as a means to provide a mechanistic understanding of metabolic vulnerabilities of tumors with the goal of enhancing tumor cell responses to radio-chemotherapies. Overall, I believe that this F99/K00 award will provide the means for me to utilize my skills as a redox biologist in order to establish a research career focused on cancer cell redox metabolism and DNA damage repair/signaling in tumor cell responses to radio- chemotherapies.

application_id: 10938549
activity_code: DP2
title: Administrative Supplement: Characterizing Macrophages as "Hide-Outs" for Chronic Pathogens
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Both HIV and Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) establish life-long infections that, despite the development of antiretroviral therapy and antibiotics, cause millions of deaths every year, emphasizing the need to develop a cure. This proposal aims to define the methods by which HIV and Mtb evade immune-mediated clearance by characterizing their common target cell, the macrophage, and how this cell acts to shield these pathogens from immune responses. Insight into these mechanisms will help identify macrophage-specific pathways that can be exploited to make these infected cells more susceptible to immune-mediated clearance, potentiating the development of cure-based strategies for HIV and Mtb infection.

title: Molecular tools to decipher communication across the blood-brain barrier
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative The blood-brain barrier impedes the effective treatment of nearly all brain disorders, and its dysfunction is a leading cause of death. I propose developing and applying new tools to tag, track, and identify which native proteins and cells in the blood can traverse the blood-brain barrier and learn the rules by which they do so. This will expand many-fold the set of therapeutic pathways into the brain, reveal new targets to block disease- provoking neuroinflammation, and provide a new understanding of how communication across the blood-brain barrier supports brain health.

title: Towards a Comprehensive, Spatiotemporal Roadmap of Cancer Metastasis
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer metastasis accounts for more than 8 million deaths worldwide annually, however, our understanding of
the mechanisms underlying metastasis remains incomplete. This proposal aims to develop a comprehensive,
spatiotemporally-resolved roadmap of small cell lung cancer metastasis by developing and applying new

application_id: 10890190
activity_code: K00
title: Investigation of tumor stem cell maintenance and cellular hierarchy in pediatric high-grade glioma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Pediatric high-grade glioma (PHGG), a deadly childhood brain tumor, likely originates from tumor stem  cells that differentiate into proliferating tumor cells. PHGG tumors are heterogeneous and include several  different types of proliferating cells, leading to the question of how tumor stem cells and proliferating tumor  cells relate to one another. I will investigate whether a single multipotent tumor stem cell population  produces the multiple proliferating cell types of PHGG, to provide a biological foundation for the  development of critically needed advances in therapeutic alternatives for this deadly childhood disease.

title: High-throughput dissection of transcriptional regulation in kidney disease

phr: Transcriptional dysregulation is common characteristic of cells in disease states, but the combinatorial complexity with which non-coding elements and chromatin regulatory proteins interact to determine gene expression makes it difficult to determine how transcriptional dysregulation arises and how it can be therapeutically reversed. title: Development of an Ultrasensitive Blood-based Diagnostic for Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a leading cause of death and an increasingly urgent health concern, as it afflicts a growing percentage of the elderly population, with observed disparities across racial and educational demographics. Objective, accurate, and reliable diagnosis of AD remains a severe unmet need, as existing diagnoses involve either lengthy and subjective clinical tests with brain scans or expensive and invasive procedures like spinal taps. application_id: 10903550
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the transition from deep quiescence to senescence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Quiescent cancer cells are a source of drug resistance as they evade chemotherapeutic treatments targeted at cycling cells and retain their ability to proliferate in the future, whereas senescent cancer cells are tumor- suppressive as they limit the proliferation of damaged cells and recruit the immune system. The proposed research will investigate how cells transit between quiescence and senescence and will determine the mechanism by which quiescent cells permanently lose their ability to proliferate, thus becoming senescent. Quiescent and senescent cells arise side-by-side following chemotherapeutic treatment, thus, a mechanistic understanding of how cells choose between these fates would aid in the development of more targeted and effective chemotherapy treatments.

activity_code: K23
title: Association of Dialysate Bicarbonate with Hemodynamic Instability and Arrhythmia
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 While approximately 450,000 patients in the United States are treated with life-sustaining maintenance
hemodialysis for end-stage renal disease, their mortality rate is unacceptably high, reaching approximately
50% by three years from the time of hemodialysis initiation. Rates of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality,
including sudden cardiac death, are higher on the days of dialysis and have been associated with higher serum
and dialysate bicarbonate concentrations. Our proposal will explore the effect of altering the dialysate
bicarbonate on intradialytic hypotension and electrocardiographic changes associated with sudden cardiac
death.

application_id: 10828447
activity_code: K99
title: Metabolic syndrome impairs oligodendrocyte metabolic support of neurons leading to Alzheimer’s disease/Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Metabolic Syndrome (MetS) and its components, such as diabetes and obesity, in mid-life are associated with
increased risk of cognitive impairment, dementia, and Alzheimer’s disease (AD) later in life. In this proposal,
we will focus on understanding how mid-life MetS onset disrupts metabolism in oligodendrocyte cells that
support neurons to define the mechanism that underlies how MetS leads to neuronal loss in the brain, and
ultimately AD, in later life. By understanding the mechanisms that contribute to neuronal loss in MetS-
associated AD, we can identify novel, targeted, translatable interventions to reduce MetS-related cognitive
decline.

application_id: 10904676
activity_code: F31
title: Solving cell-type specific differences of the Wnt-directed gene regulatory network in Hydra vulgaris.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Wnt signaling is conserved across animals and plays a role in many different developmental processes and diseases, which requires that the same signal activates different sets of genes in different contexts. It is largely unknown how it is possible for a single signal to have different effects and whether these mechanisms are conserved across species and during disease. In this study, I aim to identify the regulatory mechanism that impact the output of Wnt signaling using Hydra vulgaris, which offers the advantage of a simplified system for addressing a complex question.

application_id: 10904745
activity_code: K01
title: Optimizing implementation of long-acting, injectable Cabotegravir for HIV prevention among adolescents and young adults in Zambia
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Long-acting, injectable Cabotegravir (LAI CAB) HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) has the potential to prevent HIV among those who experience barriers to effective use of daily, oral PrEP, including many adolescents and young adults (AYA) in sub-Saharan Africa who remain at highest risk of HIV incidence. Disappointing, inequitable outcomes from the initial roll-out of oral PrEP globally and limited utilization of promising injectable formulations in other disease areas demonstrate that implementation success is far from certain, however. This study will identify LAI CAB health system implementation opportunities, measure HCW and AYA implementation preferences, identify preference heterogeneity, and specify implementation strategies to optimize LAI CAB impact in Zambia and globally.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Metabolic diseases, such as obesity, type II diabetes, and atherosclerosis are often the result of multi-system changes at the organ, cell, and intracellular level. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO

activity_code: K08
title: Network models to capture multiple outcomes resulting from multi-component, salutogenic interventions
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Health care research has extensively studied pathogenesis or disease progression, but little is known about
the complementary process of salutogenesis, or health restoration. This project will examine biochemical and
functional changes associated with the reversal of age-related cognitive decline in mice, as a result of
treatment with exercise and/or a botanical extract. Results will be used to design a computational model for
future application to the study of multiple interventions on multiple body systems in animal or human studies.

application_id: 10906795
activity_code: F30
title: Alzheimer's Disease Genetic Risk and Microglial Innate Immune Memory
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Genetic studies of Alzheimer’s disease have demonstrated a critical pathogenic role for brain immune cells known as microglia, whose function appears to be impaired. This proposal aims to investigate how genetic factors that promote Alzheimer’s disease act to impair microglial function by modifying their long-term responses to inflammatory signals. Our studies will help outline the link between inflammation and Alzheimer’s disease, potentially leading to novel approaches to therapy.

title: Identifying Proteomic Markers of Exercise Training in Heart Failure
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The defining morbidity of heart failure is exercise intolerance, and while exercise training is known to benefit
these patients, the molecular transducers of exercise’s benefit to patients are unknown. This study will
investigate the proteomic profiles of exercise training in heart failure patients and compare them to proteomic
profiles in healthy adults. The results will not only identify biological pathways for further investigation as
potential drug targets for symptom reduction, but also provide a proteomic framework for exercise training
response in heart failure.

activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the Neuronal Signals Initiating Synapse Loss in Aging and Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Cognitive decline is a clinical hallmark of late-onset Alzheimer’s disease (LOAD), costing millions of elderly individuals’ quality of life years. Synapse loss is highly correlated with cognitive decline, and seen early in LOAD and aging; while potential neuronal-microglial interactions mediating this loss have been identified, it remains unknown how these interactions are triggered in aging and confer vulnerability to LOAD. This proposal will elucidate specific upstream, age-related neuronal calcium and mitochondrial dysregulation that may trigger synapse loss, providing potential key therapeutic targets to preempt cognitive decline, and thus extending the number of healthy, active years of life.

title: Functional mechanisms of environmental control over social behavior
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Environmental conditions determine if and how individuals interact with one another; yet the complexity of the nervous system of mammals such as mice and humans has limited a mechanistic understanding of how environmental influences shape sensory processing and social behavior. application_id: 10833488
activity_code: K24
title: Mentoring Clinical Investigators in Patient Oriented Research on Human Mobility and HIV
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative There is an urgent need to better understand the geospatial and temporal dimensions of human mobility that lead to disparities in HIV outcomes, and for feasible and scalable interventions to improve HIV outcomes in mobile populations. Yet, research in these areas is nascent, and there is a great need for an expanded scientific workforce with training in human mobility and HIV to tackle these challenges. This application for the Midcareer Investigator Award in Patient-Oriented Research (K24) proposes a comprehensive mentoring, research and career development plan which will lay the groundwork for sustained, high impact research that is urgently needed to address the challenges posed by mobility to achieving HIV prevention and treatment goals.

application_id: 11184185
activity_code: DP5
title: High-throughput imaging of 3D chromatin regulation events in the nervous system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
This project aims to establish a multiplexed imaging and synthetic biology platform to
mechanistically understand chromatin regulation and its functional role in the peripheral
olfactory system. Specifically, Aim 1 is to develop a high-throughput, multi-modal imaging
method to directly visualize the 3D organization of DNA together with key regulatory proteins
and the transcriptional output controlling the fates of single-neurons in the olfactory epithelium.
Aim 2, is to deliver and image large libraries of compact viruses containing different
combinations of regulatory elements and gene promoters to enable specific transgenic control
of the different sub-populations of olfactory neuronal types.

title: Maternal immune activation remodeling of offspring glycosaminoglycan sulfation patterns during neurodevelopment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Maternal immune activation (MIA) is a primer for long-term neuropsychiatric consequences in offspring, although the exact mechanisms underlying offspring neurodevelopmental defects resulting from these maternal immune insults are unknown. Here, we provide evidence that MIA-induced changes in the developing offspring’s extracellular chondroitin and dermatan sulfate glycosaminoglycan (CS/DS-GAG) sulfation patterning, which are key regulator of axonal guidance and synaptogenesis during neurodevelopment, may result in developmental defects that predispose the offspring to mental health disorders in the adult, and studies outlined within this proposal aim to further investigate this possibility.

activity_code: K08
title: Targeting SOCS1 and RASA2 to Engineer More Potent Adoptive T Cell Therapies for Cancer Treatment.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adoptive T cell therapies have shown impressive performance in directing the immune system against some cancers, however the majority of patients still do not respond to this promising therapeutic strategy and new tools are needed to accelerate the discovery of more potent T cell therapies. application_id: 10835063
activity_code: K23
title: Development of a Pharmacodynamic Biomarker of Opioid Antagonism in Adolescents with Eating Disorders
org_name: CHILDREN'S MERCY HOSP (KANSAS CITY, MO)
phr: Project Narrative Eating disorders are common in adolescents, increase the risk of substance use, have the highest mortality rate of any psychiatric illness and are not responsive to current pharmacotherapy. The overall goal of this K23 proposal is to evaluate neuroimaging as a pharmacodynamic biomarker of opioid antagonism in adolescents with eating disorders through practical application of training in neuroimaging, pediatric randomized clinical trials and exposure-response modeling. At the completion of this proposal, Dr. Stephani Stancil will be well- positioned for an independent career as an interdisciplinary translational scientist focused on pharmacodynamic biomarker development to support early-stage drug development in pediatric neuropsychopharmacology.

application_id: 10911175
activity_code: K08
title: Integrative subtyping to improve therapeutic options for metastatic hormone receptor-positive breast cancer
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Metastatic breast cancer, of which hormone receptor-positive breast cancer is the most common, is estimated to affect approximately 150,000 women in the United States; most of these women will die of breast cancer, making it the second leading cause of cancer death among women. This proposal will examine distinct molecular categories of hormone receptor-positive metastatic breast cancer to understand whether different treatments may affect these groups differently. The knowledge gained will lay the groundwork for a personalized approach to therapy for metastatic breast cancer to improve health and lengthen life.
